REVIEW: ran a 3-angle Workflow review (correctness verified against real vendored autobase source; fake-conformance comparing _FakeBase against the real index.js+recipes handlers; schema-conventions for doc/categorization consistency), each finding independently adversarially verified. 15/15 findings CONFIRMED, all fixed:
- CRITICAL: BASE_APPEND never validated op shape before base.append(), so a malformed/wrong-recipe op was silently accepted then silently dropped by apply()'s own MALFORMED_OP-skip logic, with zero error ever reaching the caller. Fixed: BASE_APPEND now calls recipe.validate() / validateAddWriter / validateRemoveWriter synchronously before base.append(), after normalizeAppend (so crdtMap's del auto-fill still runs first).
- CRITICAL: removeWriter's doc promised a scoped PearStorageException when Autobase refuses to remove the last indexer, but tracing the pinned autobase source showed that error is UNCAUGHT by default and crashes the entire worklet process (Autobase's _onError calls crashSoon() when no 'error' listener exists on the base, confirmed in node_modules/autobase/index.js). Fixed: BASE_OPEN now registers base.on('error', ...) so _onError closes just this one base instead -- every other open core/bee/drive/base in the worklet survives. Doc updated to describe the corrected, accurate behavior.
- Real race: crdtMap's normalizeAppend does a genuine async view read with no per-base call serialization, so an overlapping put-then-del on the same key could have the del observe a stale (pre-put) view. Fixed: BASE_APPEND now chains through a per-base appendQueue so calls on one base are strictly FIFO.
- Fake-conformance (multiple): the fake's lww/crdtMap tie-break used a hub-wide, call-arrival-order counter instead of each writer's own local sequence number, so a test's fixture data could see the fake pick the opposite winner from what the real isNewer/causal-order recipe would for the identical writer history -- fixed via per-(base,writer) local counters + writer-key tiebreak, matching the real crdtMap recipe's isNewer exactly. writerKey was per-worklet instead of per-base (real base.local.key is namespaced per base) -- fixed via a deterministic per-(worklet,base) derivation. addWriter/removeWriter bypassed the writer-admission check entirely, letting a non-writer self-admit (structurally impossible in real Autobase) -- fixed by gating the whole handler on writer membership first. removeWriter had no last-indexer protection and never read the indexer flag -- fixed by tracking indexers and refusing to remove the last one. baseReplicate fired a spurious watch notification even when a merge changed nothing (e.g. two empty bases) -- fixed by gating _notify() on an actual size change, matching bee/drive's own precedent. Name-derived key hash folded in the recipe name, diverging from the real key derivation (which ignores recipe) -- fixed to match. Documented (not re-architected, per the fake's own scope) that a base merge is a permanent fusion with no re-divergence path, unlike a real repeatedly-reconciling Autobase pair.
- Docs: corrected the class doc's malformedOp claim (only put/del/append/addWriter/removeWriter validate synchronously; get/range fail with a generic storage exception instead), added the missing "Prefer Pear.base" convention sentence to open()'s doc, and switched doc references to out-of-scope symbols (PearPairing, PearStorageException) from square brackets to backticks (dartdoc [Foo] only resolves within the importing library; base.dart/schema.dart don't import pairing.dart/exceptions.dart).

Re-validated after all fixes: melos analyze clean, 127/127 Dart tests, 18/18 JS tests, bundle rebuilt and confirmed.

DEFERRED: real-device leg (two devices write offline-ish, one suspended, resume, converge to identical view on both) -- tracked centrally in flutter_pear-doi per this project's hardware-testing-deferred-to-end convention; will append there next.

title: E5.7: Autobase prebuilt merge recipes in pear-end (LWW / ordered-log / CRDT-map)
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 5 of 5, part A — the HARD one, sequenced LAST (locked). Autobase multi-writer. LOCKED (codex #2): do NOT expose generic open/apply — incompatible with zero-JS (Dart callbacks across RPC during deterministic indexing are slow/reentrant/failure-prone). Ship PREBUILT MERGE RECIPES in the pear-end; Dart selects by name.
FILES: pear-end (+autobase dep — license-check, THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES regen): recipes module implementing three named strategies.
RECIPES:
1. last-writer-wins map (key -> value, latest logical write wins; deterministic tiebreak documented)
2. ordered-log (append-only merged log, deterministic interleave)
3. crdt-map (grow/observed-remove map semantics — document EXACTLY which CRDT variant; simplest correct one wins over clever)
STEPS: implement each recipe's open/apply in JS with determinism as the test focus; register recipe names + err.codes in schema.
VALIDATION: JS-level determinism tests (node, no device): two writers, interleaved ops applied in different arrival orders converge to identical state for ALL three recipes; malformed op -> typed error not corruption.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Three named recipes implemented JS-side with documented semantics; convergence proven by arrival-order permutation tests; malformed ops fail typed; generic open/apply NOT exposed; licenses regenerated.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/autobase-recipes.js (new) -- three named Autobase merge recipes, each exporting {open(store), apply(nodes,view,host), validate(value)} plus a recipe-specific get()/tagsFor(). lww: last-writer-wins map (key->value) trusting Autobase's own deterministic causal linearization for 'latest' -- apply() does a plain unconditional view.put() per op, no recipe-level tiebreak. orderedLog: append-only merged log, same principle (plain view.append() in Autobase's given order). crdtMap: an ADD-WINS OBSERVED-REMOVE MAP (an OR-Set of (tag,value) pairs per key, tag=writerHex:seq derived automatically from Autobase's own node identity, never generated by the caller) across two named sub-views (adds/tombs) to avoid hand-rolled key-prefix ambiguity; a del names the exact tags it observed, so a concurrent not-yet-observed put survives (add-wins); multiple surviving concurrent adds resolve via an explicit (seq,writer) tiebreak, documented as safe only because it picks among an already-converged set, never used to judge causal recency. All three share a handleAddWriter/validateAddWriter helper for the {addWriter,indexer} op shape every recipe recognizes identically. Schema: PearRecipe enum (lww/orderedLog/crdtMap, .name-is-wire-value like PearSwarmState) + PearErrorCode.malformedOp (.storage category, matching every other E5 data-structure wrapper), mirrored in schema.js's new Recipe object, cross-checked in schema_test.dart.
DESIGN DECISION CAUGHT BY REVIEW, FIXED BEFORE FIRST COMMIT OF THIS NOTE: apply() must NEVER throw, by design. Verified live against the real autobase package that an exception escaping apply() propagates to Autobase's own _onError(), which unconditionally CLOSES the whole base -- and this does not self-heal on restart, since a fresh Autobase replays the identical stored, causally-final node and hits the same throw again. So the ORIGINAL design (apply() throwing MALFORMED_OP directly) would have meant one shape-invalid op from ANY writer permanently bricking every replica that ever linearizes it -- a total, unrecoverable outage, worse than the 'corruption' this ticket's own validation requirement exists to prevent. Fixed by splitting each recipe's shape-checking into a separate validate(value) function that DOES throw MALFORMED_OP (for a caller -- PearBase.put/del, E5.8 -- to validate BEFORE ever appending, catching an app bug before it reaches the shared log at all), while apply() itself only ever SKIPS a node that fails validation (no view mutation, no host.addWriter call) and continues processing the rest of the batch. A genuine storage/session failure (as opposed to a shape problem) is NOT swallowed and still propagates -- stopping the base is the safer response to an actual environmental failure, unlike a data-shape problem with one op.
TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/test/autobase-recipes.test.js (new, 14 tests, Node's built-in test runner, run via node --test test/autobase-recipes.test.js from pear-end/ -- matches this ticket's own 'JS-level, node, no device' VALIDATION requirement literally). Per recipe: a genuine two-real-Autobase-instance convergence test (concurrent racing writes resolving to the identical winner on both replicas), a validate() malformed-op test (asserts the typed MALFORMED_OP throw), and an apply()-never-crashes test (appends a malformed op through a REAL Autobase, asserts the base does not close and a subsequent valid op in the same session still applies correctly on both sides) -- directly proving the critical fix above end to end, not just by code inspection. Plus: a second lww race test with the two concurrent appends made in the OPPOSITE code order (proving the result doesn't depend on which side happened to run first in the test's own JS timeline, addressing the review's 'different arrival orders' concern); a crdtMap test with two fully-concurrent surviving puts and no delete at all, specifically exercising the isNewer tiebreak comparator the other crdtMap tests never reach; addWriter-specific validateAddWriter and apply()-skip tests. Strengthened orderedLog's convergence assertion from '>= 4 entries, A mirrors B' to an exact length + exact content-set check (a symmetric duplication bug would have passed the weaker original). replicateAndSync hardened: tracked/cleaned-up tmp dirs via a top-level after() hook (was leaking real directories on every run before, pass or fail) and t.after()-based base/store cleanup per test (was only cleaned up at a test's final line, so a thrown assertion mid-test leaked Autobase/Corestore instances) -- tried a smarter fixed-point convergence check first (system.core.length stable for 2 rounds) but found empirically it declares victory during a long, genuinely-stable-looking plateau BEFORE a piped stream's async I/O even starts delivering data, so reverted to a generous fixed round count (60, documented as a deliberate bounded heuristic, not a proof) with an explicit escape hatch for a future bigger test. melos run analyze: 0 warnings. melos run test --no-select: 110/110 (unchanged -- E5.7 touches no Dart wrapper code, only schema.dart additions). node --test test/autobase-recipes.test.js: 14/14. npm license scan: 108 pear-end/node_modules packages, all MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC. dart run flutter_pear:pack rebuilt successfully (105 modules in THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES, up from 101 -- autobase installed as a real dependency for these tests but NOT YET required by index.js itself, so its code isn't in the shipped bundle yet; E5.8 is what wires it into an actual RPC handler).
REVIEW: ran the Workflow tool (3 parallel angles -- correctness [verified against the real autobase package source, including live repros], test-rigor, schema-conventions -- 18 agents total), each finding verified via an independent adversarial pass. Result: 15 findings, ALL 15 CONFIRMED (0 refuted), all fixed:
1. CRITICAL (correctness): apply() throwing permanently bricks the base with no restart recovery -- see the DESIGN DECISION note above; fixed by splitting validate()/apply()-skips.
2-3,6,11 (correctness/test-rigor/schema-conventions, same root cause flagged by 3 independent angles): handleAddWriter had zero shape validation -- a non-hex string silently truncated to a garbage key (deterministic corruption of the writer set, not a typed error) and a non-string/non-Buffer value threw a raw uncoded TypeError. Fixed: validateAddWriter() checks a 32-byte Buffer or 64-char hex string, throwing MALFORMED_OP otherwise; covered by both a direct validateAddWriter test and an apply()-skips-it test.
4 (correctness): no withStorageErrors-style wrapper distinguishing a malformed op from a genuine storage/session failure -- addressed by design (see DESIGN DECISION note): validate() failures are swallowed by processNode, but view.put/append/host.addWriter failures are NOT, and propagate/close the base -- a real environmental failure, unlike a shape problem, and stopping is the safer response; documented explicitly in the module's own top comment. A proper RPC-layer categorization wrapper (this module's own analog of withStorageErrors) is E5.8's job once there's an actual RPC boundary.
5,12 (correctness/schema-conventions): crdtMap del's removes validation only checked each tag was a string, never that it matched the writerHex:seq shape schema.dart's own malformedOp doc explicitly promises to catch -- a fabricated tag passed validation and permanently polluted the tombs sub-view. Fixed: TAG_RE regex validates the exact shape before any mutation; new test covers it.
7 (test-rigor): only one fixed replication topology/schedule was tested per recipe, short of the ticket's own 'different arrival orders' wording. Addressed with a second lww race test using the opposite code-order for the two concurrent appends (proving the result is independent of which side's .append() literally ran first).
REVIEW (continued):
8 (test-rigor): the crdtMap isNewer tiebreak comparator -- the one piece of logic the module's own comments flagged as needing scrutiny -- was never exercised by any test (every existing scenario always ended with 0 or 1 surviving tags per key). Fixed: new test with two fully-concurrent surviving puts and no delete at all, forcing get() to actually pick a winner via isNewer.
9 (test-rigor): orderedLog's convergence check (length >= 4, A mirrors B) would not catch a symmetric duplication bug affecting both replicas identically. Fixed: exact length (4) + exact expected content-set assertion.
10 (test-rigor): missing malformed-op coverage for put-missing-value (lww, crdtMap) and crdtMap's bad-type branch. Fixed: added to the validate() test cases.
13 (test-rigor): replicateAndSync's fixed 20-round loop had no real fixed-point check, and assertion failures mid-test left bases/Corestores/tmp-dirs uncleaned. Tried a smarter convergence check (system.core.length stable for 2 rounds) and found empirically (round-by-round logging) it declares victory during a long genuinely-stable-looking plateau before the piped stream's async I/O even starts -- reverted to a generous fixed round count (60) with an honest comment about the tradeoff, plus t.after()-based cleanup for bases/stores and a top-level after() hook removing every tracked tmp dir regardless of pass/fail.
14 (schema-conventions): LICENSING.md's table wasn't updated for autobase's larger transitive tree -- hypercore-storage/rocksdb-native (Apache-2.0) and kademlia-routing-table/dht-rpc (MIT) were absent, and rocksdb-native's CMake-time fetch of external librocksdb/libjstl C++ source (outside node_modules, invisible to the JS-based THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES collector) was never mentioned. Verified both fetched repos (holepunchto/librocksdb, holepunchto/libjstl) are Apache-2.0 via the GitHub API -- confirming the safe branch of upstream RocksDB's Apache-2.0/GPLv2 dual license is what's actually used, not GPLv2. Fixed: added all four packages to the table plus a footnote explaining the CMake fetch and its license.
15 (schema-conventions): PearRecipe's doc comment pointed only to the pear-end recipes module, never to schema.js's own Recipe mirror object (the actual wire cross-check schema_test.dart enforces) -- unlike PearSwarmState's doc, which explicitly names SwarmState. Fixed: doc comment now explicitly parallels PearSwarmState's wording and names schema.js's Recipe object.
16 (schema-conventions, minor/stylistic): malformedOp() is a shared error-factory, a pattern index.js's own ~15 inline coded-throw sites never use. Addressed with an explanatory comment (not a rewrite) noting this is a deliberate DRY choice given ~15 call sites across 3 recipes throwing the identical code, unlike index.js's one-off codes.
melos run analyze + melos run test --no-select re-run clean after every fix (110/110 final, unchanged). node --test test/autobase-recipes.test.js: 14/14 final. dart run flutter_pear:pack re-run successfully (105 modules).
DEFERRED: none for this ticket specifically -- re-read the VALIDATION line before closing per this ticket's own BEFORE CLOSING checklist: it explicitly scopes this to JS-level (node, no device) determinism tests only, with no real-device leg of its own (unlike E5.1/E5.2/E5.3/E5.5/E5.6). No flutter_pear-doi update needed here. The Dart-facing wrapper (PearBase), its own fake-conformance tests, and its real-device convergence test are E5.8 (flutter_pear-2vz.8, now unblocked), which is where a hardware leg will apply.
close_reason: Implemented (3 recipes + schema), tested (14 JS-level determinism tests, 110/110 Dart), reviewed via Workflow (15/15 findings confirmed and fixed, including a critical apply()-throw-bricks-the-base design flaw caught before it shipped), licenses checked and LICENSING.md corrected. No hardware leg for this ticket per its own VALIDATION scope.

title: E5.6: PearPairing — blind-pairing wrapper (invites, device linking)
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 4 of 5. Blind pairing — invites + device linking. Feeds the example app QR flow (E7): the QR encodes an invite, not a raw topic. This is the safe alternative the unsafeTopicFromString rename (E8) steers users toward.
FILES: pear-end (+blind-pairing dep — license-check, THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES regen), packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/pairing.dart (PearPairing), schema, fake extension, tests.
API SHAPE: create invite (short shareable string/bytes with TTL semantics documented), accept invite -> paired connection/keys, revoke. Document expiry + replay posture honestly (persistence questions are pinned to M2 — do not build storage here, document what is and is not guaranteed).
VALIDATION: fake tests: create/accept round trip yields linked peers, expired/garbage invite -> typed error (not hang), revoke blocks accept. Real-device test: invite created on A, accepted on B, paired connection exchanges bytes.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Invite create/accept/revoke per API shape with documented TTL/replay posture; garbage/expired invites fail typed; fake tests + real-device pairing test green; licenses regenerated.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: PearPairing (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/pairing.dart) -- PearPairingCandidate/PearInvite/PearPairing, wrapping blind-pairing. Unlike PearStore/PearBee/PearDrive this has no open-by-name/key, no writer, no replicate() -- it is a handshake protocol, not a corestore-backed structure. createInvite(rpc, {ttl}) returns a PearInvite (invite bytes + id + a candidates broadcast Stream); acceptInvite(rpc, invite, {userData, timeout}) blocks until confirmed or timed out. confirm(key) takes a mandatory 32-byte PearKey (blind-pairing-core's ResponsePayload fixed32). Expiry is enforced by this wrapper (checked at accept time), not by blind-pairing itself -- confirmed by reading blind-pairing-core's source. pear-end/index.js: BlindPairing wired to the shared swarm; invites Map + getInviteOrThrow; PAIRING_CREATE_INVITE/CONFIRM_CANDIDATE/REVOKE/ACCEPT_INVITE handlers. Schema: 4 methods, 1 event (pairing.candidate), 5 error codes (invalidInvite/inviteExpired/pairingTimeout/unknownInvite/unknownCandidate), all mapped to PearErrorCategory.connection (the first E5 wrapper NOT categorized .storage). flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet extended: hub-shared _invites map (an invite is one resource from creation, not per-worklet-then-merged like the core/bee/drive family), _FakeInvite/_FakePendingCandidate, a revoked flag (not removed on revoke, matching real blind-pairing's decodeInvite being a pure function of the invite bytes).
TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/test/pairing_test.dart (11 tests): create/accept round trip, userData visible to inviter, garbage invite -> INVALID_INVITE, expired ttl -> INVITE_EXPIRED, revoke blocks accept -> PAIRING_TIMEOUT, never-confirmed accept -> PAIRING_TIMEOUT, unknown candidate on a real invite -> UNKNOWN_CANDIDATE, unknown invite id -> UNKNOWN_INVITE, plus 2 tests added during the post-review fix pass: revoke-then-confirm -> UNKNOWN_INVITE (not stale success) with the pending accept still timing out, and a different worklet cannot confirm someone else's candidate (raw RPC call, bypassing the typed API) -> UNKNOWN_INVITE with the pending accept still timing out. melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 110/110 in flutter_pear (was 108 before this ticket's post-review fixes, 100 before E5.6 started), 13/13 flutter_pear_test. dart run flutter_pear:pack rebuilt the bundle successfully (101 modules in THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES). License check: 105 pear-end/node_modules packages scanned, all MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC; blind-pairing-core added to LICENSING.md's Apache-2.0 column; protomux/compact-encoding promoted from transitive to direct pear-end dependencies (both already listed in LICENSING.md's table, no doc change needed there).
REVIEW: ran the Workflow tool (3 parallel angles -- js-dart-correctness, fake-conformance, schema-conventions -- 13 agents total), each finding verified via an independent adversarial pass. Result: 10 findings, ALL 10 CONFIRMED (0 refuted), all fixed:
1. CRITICAL (js-dart-correctness): BlindPairing wraps EVERY peer connection in a Protomux instance from worklet boot (its own swarm.on(connection) listener, unconditional, not just while pairing is active). Method.CONNECTION_WRITE/CONNECTION_DATA (the raw app-data channel from E1-E4) used conn.write()/conn.on(data) directly on that SAME stream -- a second raw listener that Protomux's own _ondata sees and, on any byte sequence it can't parse as its own framing, destroys the WHOLE connection (verified by tracing protomux's _ondata -> _safeDestroy -> stream.destroy). Fixed: CONNECTION_WRITE/DATA now go through their own Protomux channel (protocol pear-connection-data) via Protomux.from(conn), which reuses BlindPairing's own cached stream.userData instance instead of a second conflicting listener -- the same coexistence pattern Hypercore's own replicate() already relies on. protomux + compact-encoding promoted to direct pear-end deps.
2. (js-dart-correctness): request.open(publicKey) in PAIRING_CREATE_INVITE's onadd threw unhandled on a malformed/tampered pairing request, propagating through Protomux's per-message rejection into destroying the whole connection instead of just rejecting one bad candidate. Fixed: wrapped in try/catch, drops just that candidate (diagnostic + resolve, no candidate event) on failure.
3. (js-dart-correctness): PAIRING_CONFIRM_CANDIDATE deleted the candidate entry BEFORE calling confirm() -- confirm() can throw synchronously on a non-32-byte key (Dart's PearKey length check is assert-only, stripped in release builds), permanently discarding the candidate so a retry got UNKNOWN_CANDIDATE instead of succeeding. Fixed: confirm() first, delete only on success.
4. (js-dart-correctness): PAIRING_REVOKE could hang forever awaiting member.close() if a candidate was announced (PAIRING_CANDIDATE sent) but never confirmed -- blind-pairing's own Member._addRequest awaits that candidate's onadd promise before its poll loop can terminate. Fixed: resolve all pending candidates (without confirming) before closing the member -- their own acceptInvite() still times out via its own bound, same as any other revoked invite.
5. (fake-conformance): FakeBareWorklet's pairingConfirmCandidate never checked _FakeInvite.revoked, so a candidate pending before a revoke could still be confirmed successfully after -- diverges from the real worklet, where PAIRING_REVOKE deletes the invite outright so a post-revoke confirm hits UNKNOWN_INVITE. Fixed: checks revoked (and ownership, see #6) before touching pending.
6. (fake-conformance): pairingConfirmCandidate had no check that the calling worklet created the invite -- hub._invites is shared hub-wide with no per-worklet privacy, unlike the real per-process invites Map. Fixed: added an identical(invite.owner, this) check alongside #5's revoked check, both throwing UNKNOWN_INVITE (matching real behavior). Both regression-tested.
REVIEW (continued):
7. (schema-conventions): schema.dart documented UNKNOWN_INVITE as throwable by both pairingConfirmCandidate AND pairingRevoke, but pairingRevoke is deliberately idempotent (matches pairing.dart's own PearInvite.revoke doc) and never throws it. Fixed: corrected the doc comment, no behavior change.
8. (schema-conventions): all four E5.6 handlers used withStorageErrors, whose uncoded-error fallback stamps STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE -- mapped to PearErrorCategory.storage -- even though every explicit E5.6 code is deliberately .connection. An uncoded pairing failure (e.g. #3's bad-length key) would have surfaced as the wrong exception type (PearStorageException instead of PearConnectionException). Fixed: added a new PAIRING_FAILED error code (.connection category) plus a withPairingErrors wrapper (E5.6's analog of withStorageErrors), switched all four handlers to it.
9. (schema-conventions): const timeoutMs = p.timeoutMs || 30000 treated an explicit 0ms timeout (fail-fast) the same as omitted, since 0 is falsy in JS -- silently overriding it to 30s. Fixed: p.timeoutMs != null ? p.timeoutMs : 30000.
10. (schema-conventions): the invites Map's shape comment was stale (claimed a publicKey field and resolveConfirmed key that were never actually stored -- real keys are member/candidates and request/resolve). Fixed: comment corrected to match actual runtime shape.
Also caught while writing this closing summary (bd-usage, not code): flutter_pear-doi's tracking notes had silently lost E5.3's deferred hardware-test entry because an earlier session used --notes (replace) instead of --append-notes (append) on it -- recovered the lost entry and recorded this as a feedback memory (feedback_bd_notes_append) so it doesn't recur.
melos run analyze + melos run test --no-select re-run clean after every fix above (110/110 final). dart run flutter_pear:pack re-run successfully after the fixes (101 modules, unchanged).
DEFERRED (per this project's standing automated-tests-first, hardware-last decision -- same treatment as E1.5/E5.1/E5.2/E5.3/E5.5): this ticket's VALIDATION also calls for a real-device test (invite created on A, accepted on B, paired connection exchanges bytes) -- deferred and tracked centrally in flutter_pear-doi, not silently dropped. Additionally flagged there as HIGH PRIORITY and distinct from the pairing round trip itself: finding #1's fix (routing CONNECTION_WRITE/DATA through a Protomux channel to coexist with BlindPairing) touches pear-end/index.js's real JS, which no Dart test exercises at the wire level (FakeBareWorklet is a pure in-memory double with no real Protomux/Node stream framing) -- this fix is architecturally sound by inspection and confirmed by the review's adversarial verification pass, but genuinely UNVERIFIED until the final hardware pass actually runs a live connection carrying both raw app data and a pairing handshake. Everything else in ACCEPTANCE (create/accept/revoke API shape, expiry+replay posture documented honestly, fake tests covering happy+error+ordering paths, licenses regenerated) is fully met now.
close_reason: Implemented, tested (110/110 green), reviewed via Workflow (10/10 findings confirmed and fixed), bundle rebuilt, licenses checked. Real-device leg deferred to flutter_pear-doi.

title: E5.5: PearDrive — Hyperdrive wrapper on the benchmarked bulk path
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 3 of 5 — the BULK-RISK wrapper. Hyperdrive (files). LOCKED transport: whatever E5.1's decision record chose (default file-path import/export via the E4.4 seam; NO in-channel streaming without benchmark justification).
FILES: pear-end (+hyperdrive/localdrive/mirror deps — license-check, THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES regen), packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/drive.dart (PearDrive), schema, fake extension, tests.
API SHAPE: put/get file (by path), list, exists, delete, mirror-to-disk; get returns a File path or bytes per the transport decision — document which and why in dartdoc.
MEMORY CRITERION (plan success criteria): a transfer large enough to require chunking completes WITHOUT exhausting memory — the file-path route must stream on the JS side, never buffer whole payloads in the RPC envelope.
VALIDATION: fake tests (put/get/list/delete + error paths: missing file, deleted drive); real-device test: transfer a >=64MB file device A -> B over replication while dumping Dart+native memory stats — no OOM, no full-file JSON frame (assert via discriminator/frame-size guard).

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: File ops per API shape on the E5.1-chosen transport; >=64MB device-to-device transfer completes without OOM and provably never rides a JSON frame; fake+error tests green; licenses regenerated.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: PearDrive (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/drive.dart, new) -- a single class (matching project_plan.md's table: Hyperdrive+localdrive/mirror -> PearDrive, one class, same shape as E5.3's PearBee, not a two-class split like E5.2's PearStore/PearCore) with a static PearDrive.open(rpc, {name, key}) factory. put/get/exists/delete/list/replicate/mirrorToDisk/close. Every put/get moves bytes by LOCAL FILE PATH ONLY -- the E5.1-benchmarked, LOCKED transport decision (BENCHMARK.md) -- never as in-memory Uint8List, satisfying the MEMORY CRITERION (a transfer large enough to require chunking must not exhaust memory): the worklet streams straight between the local filesystem and the drive on both ends via bare-fs's createReadStream/createWriteStream piped (via streamx's pipelinePromise) into the drive's own createReadStream/createWriteStream, so the RPC envelope only ever carries two path strings, never file bytes, regardless of file size.

pear-end/index.js: hyperdrive ^13.3.2 + localdrive ^2.2.1 + mirror-drive ^1.14.2 added (all Apache-2.0, license-checked alongside streamx MIT -- 101 resolved node_modules packages scanned, all MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC). DRIVE_REPLICATE replicates BOTH of a drive's underlying cores (the metadata/path-listing core AND the separate Hyperblobs content core -- verified via hyperdrive's own source that file bytes live in a distinct core from the path listing, so replicating only one would sync listings with no bytes ever following), chained through the same shared replicationStreams map as E5.2/E5.3's core/bee replication. DRIVE_MIRROR_TO_DISK reuses hyperdrive's own .mirror() (returns a real mirror-drive MirrorDrive instance) against a Localdrive -- no hand-rolled diff/copy logic. flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet extended to conform: real dart:io file I/O for put/get/mirrorToDisk (unlike E5.2/E5.3's purely in-memory fakes, this one genuinely touches temp files since that's the whole point of the API), the same writer-tracking + per-worklet name-salting + two-sided replicate handshake + subset-safety-check-before-merge pattern as E5.2/E5.3 (widened _isSubsetWithMatchingValues to accept a plain Map so both bee and drive merges reuse it).

TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/test/drive_test.dart (new, 16 tests): put/get round-trip, overwrite, exists/delete (this ticket's VALIDATION named put/get/list/delete explicitly), list ordering + folder-scoping, FILE_NOT_FOUND and DRIVE_CLOSED error paths (VALIDATION's "missing file, deleted drive"), mirrorToDisk, reopen-after-close, per-worklet name-key non-collision, writer-only-write enforcement, one-sided-replicate-never-syncs, and a full two-peer replicate flow (A puts a file before replicating, B attaches by key, both replicate, B gets the file's actual bytes back, then sees a second file A puts afterward). melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 100/100 flutter_pear (was 84 before this ticket), 13/13 flutter_pear_test, 6/6 flutter_pear_bare -- all green. dart run flutter_pear:pack rebuilt the real bundle successfully with hyperdrive/localdrive/mirror-drive linked (98 modules in THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES), including confirming localdrive's own `require('fs')`/`require('fs/promises')` (Node-style, not `bare-fs`) resolves correctly under bare-pack's builtin mapping -- a real compatibility question this ticket's implementation phase specifically had to verify, not just assume.

REVIEW: ran the review through the Workflow tool (3 parallel angles -- JS/Dart correctness, fake-worklet conformance, schema/conventions -- each verified via an independent adversarial pass, per this ticket's BEFORE CLOSING step 6). schema-conventions found nothing beyond the license-doc gap below; fake-conformance's one candidate finding (raw dart:io calls in the fake having no catch-all for genuinely unexpected I/O failures) was REFUTED on verification. js-dart-correctness surfaced 3 real, CONFIRMED bugs, all fixed:
- CRITICAL: DRIVE_OPEN's name-derived path opened a scratch Hypercore session purely to read its .key, then never closed it -- verified against the real hypercore/corestore source that every session pushes itself into a shared, never-auto-pruned session array, so this leaked one session per name-based drive.open call for the life of the worklet, with no cleanup path (not even via PearDrive.close(), which only touches the Hyperdrive's own sessions). Fixed: the scratch session is now closed immediately after reading its key.
- CRITICAL (compounding the above): reopening an already-registered drive name/key unconditionally constructed AND fully opened (.ready()'d) a second Hyperdrive -- verified this eagerly opens its own db AND blobs sessions too (3 more leaked sessions per redundant open) -- then silently discarded it outside the registration guard, unlike the sibling BEE_OPEN handler which already deferred its own construction inside the guard. Fixed: restructured DRIVE_OPEN so the (heavier) Hyperdrive construction + ready() only happens once, inside the "not yet registered" guard, matching BEE_OPEN's already-correct pattern.
- MEDIUM (accepted, documented, not code-fixed): DRIVE_GET's exists()-then-createReadStream() has a narrow TOCTOU race -- a concurrent delete in that window surfaces the library's own uncoded "blob does not exist" error (mapped to STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE by withStorageErrors) instead of the more precise FILE_NOT_FOUND. Documented as an accepted, bounded tradeoff, same class as CORE_CLOSED's own documented race in E5.2 -- never silent, just less exact than the simple non-concurrent case this check exists for.
- LOW (fixed): streamx (used directly for pipelinePromise) was only resolving via transitive hoisting from hyperdrive/hyperbee/corestore, not declared as an explicit pear-end dependency, and missing from LICENSING.md's table. Fixed: added explicitly to package.json and to LICENSING.md's MIT column.
DISCOVERED, tracked separately: the review noted STORE_GET/BEE_OPEN (E5.2/E5.3, already closed) share a LESSER version of the first bug above (a freshly-fetched core silently discarded, unclosed, on a redundant reopen) -- out of scope to retroactively fix inside this ticket, so filed flutter_pear-0md to track hardening those two call sites separately.
Re-ran melos analyze + melos run test --no-select + a fresh dart run flutter_pear:pack after every fix -- green throughout, final state above.

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision, same treatment as E1.5/E5.1/E5.2/E5.3): this ticket's VALIDATION also calls for "real-device test: transfer a >=64MB file device A -> B over replication while dumping Dart+native memory stats -- no OOM, no full-file JSON frame." That leg is physical two-device hardware, deferred and tracked in flutter_pear-doi -- not silently dropped. Everything else in ACCEPTANCE (file ops per API shape on the E5.1-chosen transport, fake+error tests green, licenses regenerated) is fully met now; the memory-safety GUARANTEE itself is structurally proven by the streaming design (verified line-by-line against the real hyperdrive/bare-fs/streamx source during review, not just asserted), independent of the deferred large-file device test.
close_reason: E5.5 done: PearDrive (Hyperdrive file wrapper 3 of 5) implemented end-to-end -- put/get/exists/delete/list/replicate/mirrorToDisk, streaming file-to-file on both ends (bare-fs <-> drive streams via pipelinePromise) so the RPC envelope only ever carries path strings, structurally satisfying the memory-safety criterion regardless of file size. 16 new tests (100/100 green in flutter_pear). Workflow-orchestrated review (3 angles, adversarial verify) found and fixed 2 critical session-leak bugs in DRIVE_OPEN (a scratch Hypercore session never closed, and a redundant reopen silently discarding a second fully-opened Hyperdrive with its own db+blobs sessions) plus a missing explicit streamx dependency/license-doc entry; documented one narrow, accepted TOCTOU race in DRIVE_GET's error precision. Filed flutter_pear-0md to track a related, lesser version of the same leak pattern found (but out of scope to fix here) in E5.2/E5.3's already-closed STORE_GET/BEE_OPEN. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack with hyperdrive/localdrive/mirror-drive linked for Android, also confirming localdrive's Node-style fs requires resolve fine under Bare. Deps license-checked (MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC only, LICENSING.md updated). The >=64MB two-device physical-replicate leg of VALIDATION is deferred with E1.5/E5.1/E5.2/E5.3 under flutter_pear-doi, per this project's automated-first/hardware-last decision -- not silently dropped.

title: E5.3: PearBee — Hyperbee KV wrapper end-to-end
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 2 of 5. Hyperbee (KV) on the Hypercore substrate. Same hand-written pattern as E5.2. Range streams are the first real consumer of the schema's event-stream subscribe/unsubscribe primitive — implement subscription lifecycle properly (unsubscribe on Dart stream cancel; no leaked JS listeners).
FILES: pear-end (+hyperbee dep, license-check + regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES), packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/bee.dart (PearBee), schema additions, fake extension, tests.
API SHAPE: get/put/del, range read as Stream (bounded snapshot semantics documented), watch as broadcast Stream. Uint8List values, PearKey-friendly keys, dispose().
VALIDATION: fake tests: put/get/del, range over N entries in order, watch fires on change, cancel unsubscribes (assert via fake hook that the JS-side listener count drops); error paths (get on closed bee). ONE real-device test: put on A, watch fires on B over replication.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: get/put/del/range/watch per API shape; stream cancel provably unsubscribes JS-side; fake tests cover happy+error+ordering; real-device watch-over-replication test passes; licenses regenerated.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: PearBee (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/bee.dart, new) -- a single class (not a two-class PearStore/PearCore-style split, matching project_plan.md section 4's table listing "Hyperbee | PearBee" as one class) with a static PearBee.open(rpc, {name, key}) factory mirroring PearSwarm.join's precedent. get/put/del as Futures, range() as a bounded-snapshot Stream (whole result fetched in one request/response then emitted via an async* generator -- documented as NOT live, matching the locked no-chunked-streaming-yet decision), watch() as a broadcast Stream using the swarm.join/leave subscribe-lifecycle pattern (bee.watch/bee.unwatch RPC methods + a bee.update event tagged with a caller-generated watchId, since a bee can have >1 concurrent watch). replicate() added beyond the ticket's shorthand API-shape line -- the ticket's own real-device VALIDATION ("put on A, watch fires on B over replication") is only achievable if the underlying core actually replicates, so this mirrors PearCore.replicate exactly, including reusing E5.2's shared replicationStreams chaining so a future core+bee pair can multiplex one connection safely.

pear-end/index.js: hyperbee ^2.27.3 added (license-checked: all 90 resolved node_modules packages MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC, matching project convention); bees/closedBees/beeWatchers registries; BEE_WATCH consumes a Hyperbee Watcher's async-iterator in a detached loop, forwarding each change as a tagged bee.update event. Applied E5.2's review lessons proactively this time: exactly-one-of-name/key validation, correct reopen-after-close guard (!registry.has || closedSet.has, not just the former), withStorageErrors wrapping every handler. Schema: 9 new PearMethod entries, 1 event, 2 error codes (unknownBee/beeClosed), all mirrored in schema.js and cross-checked in schema_test.dart. flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet extended: _FakeBee (ordered SplayTreeMap keyed by hex-encoded raw bytes -- verified equivalent to byte-lexicographic comparison including differing-length keys), writer-only put/del enforcement, per-worklet name salting, and the same two-sided replicate handshake as _FakeCore, plus a KV-analogue safety check (subset-with-matching-values instead of prefix) before any merge.

TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/test/bee_test.dart (new, 21 tests): put/get/del happy path, range ordering + gte/lt bounds + reverse/limit (this ticket's VALIDATION named all three), watch-fires-on-change, cancel-unsubscribes-worklet-side (asserted via a new FakeBareWorklet.activeBeeWatchCount introspection hook -- the ticket's VALIDATION explicitly asked for this), BEE_CLOSED error path (get AND put on a closed bee), reopen-after-close, per-worklet name-key non-collision, writer-only-put enforcement, one-sided-replicate-never-syncs, and a full two-peer replicate flow (A puts before replicating, B attaches by key, both replicate, B reads A's data and sees a live watch update for a post-replicate put). schema_test.dart extended for the 9 new methods/1 event/2 error codes. melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 84/84 in flutter_pear (was 63 before E5.2+E5.3 this session), 13/13 flutter_pear_test, 6/6 flutter_pear_bare -- all green. dart run flutter_pear:pack rebuilt the real bundle successfully with hyperbee's dependencies linked (87 modules in THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES, up from 80 after E5.2's corestore alone).

REVIEW: ran the review through the Workflow tool (3 parallel angles -- JS/Dart correctness, fake-worklet conformance, schema/conventions -- each verified via an independent adversarial pass as its own review completed, per this ticket's BEFORE CLOSING step 6). schema-conventions came back clean (0 findings). The other two surfaced 3 real, CONFIRMED bugs, all fixed:
- CRITICAL (js-dart-correctness): watch()'s watchId was generated ONCE per watch() call, not per listen cycle. A cancel-then-immediately-relisten on the SAME Stream (e.g. a rebuilt widget) fires onCancel then onListen in that order, but a broadcast StreamController's onCancel is NOT awaited by subscription.cancel() (confirmed empirically: cancel()'s returned Future resolves before onCancel's async body finishes) -- so the stale onCancel's bee.unwatch call was still in flight when the fresh onListen reused the identical watchId, and pear-end's BEE_WATCH unconditionally overwrote its own registry entry with no close -- the OLD watcher leaked forever and the stale unwatch, arriving after, killed the FRESH watch instead. Fixed: watchId is now generated fresh inside onListen every cycle, captured into a local before onCancel's first await (avoiding a second race where a later onListen could reassign the outer variable before the paused onCancel resumes). New regression test added.
- MEDIUM (js-dart-correctness): BEE_UNWATCH resolved purely by watchId with no check that the entry's bee matches the caller's p.bee, unlike BEE_CLOSE's cleanup loop which does scope by bee -- a mismatched pair would silently close the wrong bee's watch with no error. Fixed (both real index.js and the fake, for conformance): throws UNKNOWN_BEE on a bee/watchId mismatch, matching the already-scoped BEE_CLOSE. New regression test added (drives the raw RPC methods directly, since PearBee's own public API never constructs a mismatched pair).
- HIGH (fake-conformance): _FakeBeeWatch.bee was final and never repointed during a replicate merge -- Set membership moved to the canonical shared bee correctly, but the watch's OWN .bee reference still pointed at the orphaned pre-merge object, so a later beeUnwatch/beeClose was a no-op on dead state, permanently leaking the watch (spurious future events, an uncollectable worklet reference) while FakeBareWorklet.activeBeeWatchCount reported 0 and masked it. Fixed: .bee is now mutable and repointed to canonical during the merge. New regression test added (extends the existing two-peer replicate test: cancel B's post-merge watch and confirm activeBeeWatchCount actually drops to 0).
Re-ran melos analyze + melos run test --no-select after every fix -- green throughout, final counts above.

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision -- same treatment as E1.5/E5.1/E5.2): this ticket's VALIDATION also calls for "ONE real-device test: put on A, watch fires on B over replication." That leg is physical two-device hardware, deferred and tracked centrally in flutter_pear-doi -- not silently dropped. Everything else in ACCEPTANCE (get/put/del/range/watch per API shape, stream-cancel provably unsubscribes JS-side, fake tests cover happy+error+ordering, licenses regenerated) is fully met now.
close_reason: E5.3 done: PearBee (Hyperbee KV wrapper 2 of 5) implemented end-to-end -- get/put/del, bounded range() Stream, live watch() broadcast Stream with proper subscribe/unsubscribe lifecycle, and replicate() (added beyond the ticket's API-shape shorthand since the real-device VALIDATION scenario needs it). 21 new tests (84/84 green in flutter_pear) covering every VALIDATION item: range ordering + bounds, watch-fires-on-change, cancel-unsubscribes-worklet-side (new activeBeeWatchCount introspection hook), and BEE_CLOSED/error paths. Workflow-orchestrated review (3 angles, adversarial verify) found and fixed 1 critical + 1 high + 1 medium real bug: a watchId-reuse race that could kill a fresh watch and leak the stale one on rapid cancel-then-relisten, a missing bee-ownership check on unwatch, and a fake-conformance gap where a replicated watch's bee reference never repointed to the canonical object post-merge (silent leak). All three got regression tests. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack with hyperbee linked for Android (87 modules, up from 80). Deps license-checked (MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC only). The two-device physical-replicate leg of VALIDATION is deferred with E1.5/E5.1/E5.2 under flutter_pear-doi, per this project's automated-first/hardware-last decision.

title: E5.2: PearStore/PearCore — Corestore+Hypercore wrapper end-to-end
description:
CONTEXT: E5 wrapper 1 of 5 — the SUBSTRATE (locked order: Corestore/Hypercore first; Hyperbee/Hyperdrive/Autobase sit on them). Hand-write against the schema (locked: NO codegen until after wrapper 3's assessment). Pattern for every wrapper: expose JS module over RPC -> typed Dart wrapper -> extend fake -> tests (fake + real device). Never reimplement protocol logic in Dart.
FILES: pear-end/index.js(+corestore/hypercore deps — license-check MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC, reject copyleft, regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES via pack), new packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/store.dart (PearStore, PearCore), schema additions, flutter_pear_test fake extension, tests.
API SHAPE (project_plan section 4): PearStore (open/get core by key), PearCore: append, get(i), length, replicate over a PearConnection, watch/update events. Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, Uint8List payloads, PearKey keys, dispose(), dartdoc on every symbol. Storage path per-platform via worklet app-storage.
ERRORS: register storage err.codes -> PearStorageException routing; catalog entries (problem+cause+fix+anchor) added when E8 catalog exists — leave codes registered + TODO markers otherwise.
VALIDATION: unit tests on fake (append/get/length/watch happy + error paths incl. get out-of-range, closed-core write); ONE real-device integration test: append on device A, replicate over swarm, read on device B (guards fake/real drift — codex #9).

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Typed PearStore/PearCore per plan API shape, green on fake day-one incl. error paths; real-device replicate test passes (A appends, B reads over swarm); deps license-checked; storage err.codes registered.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: PearStore/PearCore (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/store.dart, new) -- PearStore.get({name, key}) opens/attaches a Hypercore; PearCore.append/get/replicate/close, plus a length getter + updates broadcast stream (matches PearSwarm.currentState/state's established shape). Pear.store getter wired in pear.dart. Schema additions (schema.dart + pear-end/schema.js, mirrored): PearMethod.storeGet/coreAppend/coreGet/coreReplicate/coreClose, PearEventName.coreUpdate, PearErrorCode.indexOutOfRange/coreClosed/unknownCore (all registered in the storage category). pear-end/index.js: a module-level Corestore rooted at Bare.argv[0] (same storage-path rationale as E4.4's bulk seam), a cores/closedCores registry, and per-peer replicationStreams chaining (Hypercore's replicate() must chain through a prior call's returned stream, not the raw connection twice, so a future second core -- e.g. E5.3's Hyperbee -- can share a connection safely). corestore ^7.11.0 added to pear-end/package.json; license-checked (all 82 resolved node_modules packages scanned programmatically: MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC only, matching LICENSING.md's existing corestore/hypercore footnote). flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet extended to conform: store/core RPC handling, an in-memory _FakeCore data object, single-writer enforcement (only a name:-derived open can append), and a two-sided replicate handshake (merge only completes once BOTH peers have called replicate() for a (key,peer) pair, matching real Hypercore).

TESTS: packages/flutter_pear/test/store_test.dart (new, 15 tests): open-by-name, idempotent naming, append/get happy path, INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE and CORE_CLOSED error paths (both named in this ticket's VALIDATION), updates/watch semantics, reopen-after-close, per-worklet name-key non-collision, writer-only-append enforcement, one-sided-replicate-never-syncs, and a full two-peer replicate flow (A appends before replicating, B attaches by key, both replicate, B reads A's data and later sees a live update for a post-replicate append). schema_test.dart extended for the 5 new methods/1 event/3 error codes (both the Dart-side and the JS-mirror regex checks). melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 63/63 in flutter_pear (was 59), 13/13 in flutter_pear_test, 6/6 in flutter_pear_bare -- all green. `dart run flutter_pear:pack` rebuilt the real bundle successfully with corestore's ~22 new packages linked (bare-pack --preset android), proving the dependency actually bundles/links for Android, not just unit-tests clean.

REVIEW: non-trivial diff (schema + pear-end/index.js + new wrapper), so ran 3 parallel independent review agents (JS/Dart correctness, fake-worklet conformance, schema/conventions consistency) per this ticket's BEFORE CLOSING step 6. All 3 returned substantive findings, not rubber-stamps:
- CRITICAL (JS/Dart correctness, verified against the real corestore package): STORE_GET's reopen-after-close guard only re-registered a session when `!cores.has(keyHex)` -- since `cores` is never pruned on close, reopening the SAME key after CORE_CLOSE hit the guard's false branch, so closedCores.delete() never ran and the stale, permanently-closed session stayed registered forever. Fixed: the guard also re-registers whenever `closedCores.has(keyHex)` is true. Regression test added (store_test.dart).
- MEDIUM (same agent): none of the 5 new handlers wrapped genuine Corestore/Hypercore I/O failures the way BULK_WRITE_FILE already does, so a real storage failure would surface as an uncategorized base PearException instead of the PearStorageException schema.dart's own doc comment promises. Fixed: a withStorageErrors(fn) wrapper stamps STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE onto any caught error that doesn't already have a `.code` (every error this file throws intentionally already has one, so nothing already-coded gets overwritten).
- MEDIUM (same agent): PearStore.get()'s exactly-one-of-name/key precondition was only a Dart `assert` (stripped in release builds); an unfiltered neither-given call reached real corestore and threw an opaque, uncoded "Could not derive discovery from input". Fixed: STORE_GET now validates this itself and throws a clean STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE-coded error regardless of build mode -- mirrored in the fake for conformance.
- LOW (same agent, accepted as documented tradeoff, not fixed): CORE_CLOSE marks closed before awaiting core.close(), so a call already past its open-check when close begins races teardown and gets an uncoded-then-STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE error rather than the more precise CORE_CLOSED. Deemed acceptable for a first substrate wrapper rather than adding per-core operation locking.
- CRITICAL (fake-conformance agent): the replicate() merge picked "more blocks wins" with zero validation, silently discarding the losing side's real data (and firing no event at all on a length tie) if two peers had ever appended independently before replicating. Root-caused to a deeper gap: the fake let ANY worklet append to ANY core regardless of ownership, whereas real Hypercore only allows the corestore that originally derived a core (via store.get(name:)) to write it -- a key:-attached remote core is read-only. Fixed at the root: _FakeCore now tracks a `writer` (only a name:-derived open claims it), coreAppend throws STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE from a non-owning worklet, and the merge step additionally throws a loud StateError (not a silent discard) if it ever sees two non-prefix core views anyway, as a backstop.
- HIGH (same agent): store.get(name:)'s key derivation had no per-worklet salt, so two DIFFERENT FakeBareWorklet instances calling store.get(name: sameName) collided onto the identical key -- a real conformance violation (real Corestore derives name-based keys from that corestore's own per-device primary key) and the direct enabler of the finding above. Fixed: the fake now salts with the worklet's own peerKey.
- MEDIUM (same agent): the fake fully merged and synced the moment ONE side called coreReplicate; real Hypercore requires both ends to attach their own protocol instance before any data crosses over, so a test forgetting one side's call would falsely pass against the fake and hang against the real worklet. Fixed: a per-(key,peer) replicate-offered handshake now gates the merge on both sides having called it.
- MINOR (schema/conventions agent): PearErrorCode.unknownCore's dartdoc didn't mention coreClose also throws it. Fixed (one line).
All 4 new regression tests (reopen, name-key non-collision, writer-only-append, one-sided-replicate) map directly to these fixes. Re-ran melos analyze + melos run test --no-select after every fix -- green throughout, final state above.

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision -- same treatment as E1.5): this ticket's VALIDATION also calls for "ONE real-device integration test: append on device A, replicate over swarm, read on device B." That leg is explicitly physical two-device hardware, so it's deferred and tracked centrally in flutter_pear-doi (new ticket, filed this session) alongside E1.5 and E5.1's physical-device benchmark leg -- NOT silently dropped. Everything else in this ticket's ACCEPTANCE (typed API per plan shape, green fake-driven suite incl. error paths, deps license-checked, storage err.codes registered) is fully met now.

DISCOVERED, tracked separately: flutter_pear-pcg's scope extended (bd update) to note E5.2's JS handlers have the same "no JS-level unit test" gap as E4.4's join/leave logic -- worth noting the critical reopen bug above was caught by a human/agent line-by-line read, not any automated test (fake or real), which is exactly the kind of miss a real JS harness would catch mechanically.
close_reason: E5.2 done: PearStore/PearCore (Corestore/Hypercore wrapper 1 of 5) implemented end-to-end -- typed Dart API, pear-end JS handlers, schema entries, and a conforming fake, with 15 new tests (63/63 green in flutter_pear) covering append/get/length/watch happy path plus the INDEX_OUT_OF_RANGE and CORE_CLOSED error paths this ticket's VALIDATION named. 3 parallel independent reviews surfaced and got fixed: 1 critical real bug (a reopened-after-close core was permanently locked out), 1 critical + 2 supporting fake-conformance gaps (silent data-loss on merge, cross-worklet key collision, one-sided replicate falsely succeeding) rooted in a missing single-writer invariant now enforced, plus consistent STORAGE_UNAVAILABLE error-wrapping. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack with corestore linked for Android, proving it actually bundles, not just unit-tests clean. Deps license-checked (MIT/Apache/BSD/ISC only). The two-device physical-replicate leg of VALIDATION is deferred with E1.5/E5.1 under new tracking ticket flutter_pear-doi, per this project's automated-first/hardware-last decision -- not silently dropped.

title: E5.1: Platform-channel throughput benchmark + bulk-transport decision record
description:
CONTEXT: E5 prerequisite (locked, codex #4 + project_plan.md section 7): MEASURE platform-channel throughput before any bulk-transport design. The result decides Hyperdrive's data path: file-path import/export (default) vs pull-based bounded reads vs FFI escalation.
FILES: new packages/flutter_pear_example/test_driver/ or a benchmarks/ dir; short markdown decision record.
STEPS:
1. Benchmark harness over the real worklet (needs E4.4): round-trip throughput + latency for 1KB/64KB/1MB/16MB payloads through the BasicMessageChannel, and the same payloads via the file-path seam.
2. Run on one physical device + one emulator; capture numbers.
3. Write the decision record (BENCHMARK.md or bead comment): numbers table + the chosen Hyperdrive default with rationale. LOCKED default is file-path unless numbers argue otherwise; in-channel streaming requires this record as justification.
VALIDATION: harness runs repeatably (documented command); decision record committed and linked from the epic via bd comment.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Reproducible harness measures channel vs file-path across 1KB-16MB on device+emulator; committed decision record picks the Hyperdrive path with numbers; no streaming built without this justification.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION: packages/flutter_pear_example/integration_test/bulk_transport_benchmark_test.dart (new) -- a real on-device/emulator harness measuring round-trip throughput for 1KB/64KB/1MB/16MB payloads via both candidate transports: in-channel (new debug-only PearMethod.debugEcho -- a raw JSON RPC echo with no other JS-side work, added specifically so the benchmark isolates channel cost from any application-level processing) and file-path (existing PearMethod.bulkWriteFile, E4.4). The harness constructs a SECOND PearRpc sharing Pear.start()'s own worklet (verified safe by review: BareWorklet.incoming/onCrash are genuine broadcast streams, PearRpc matches responses by its own per-instance request id so a foreign response is a silent no-op, and PearRpc.dispose() never touches the shared controllers).

RESULTS (packages/flutter_pear/BENCHMARK.md, full table + analysis): 3 runs on an Android emulator, reproducible. 1KB/64KB are noise-dominated/comparable; 1MB and 16MB show file-path consistently ~2-2.5x faster (16MB: ~1.0s file-path vs ~2.6s in-channel, every run). DECISION: LOCKED default (file-path) CONFIRMED -- the numbers actively support it, not just fail to contradict it. PearDrive (E5.5) should default file transfers through a bulk.writeFile-style seam.

TESTS/VALIDATION: melos run analyze: 0 warnings across all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 84/84 flutter_pear, 13/13 flutter_pear_test, 6/6 flutter_pear_bare -- unaffected, all green. schema_test.dart extended for PearMethod.debugEcho (both the Dart constant and the JS-mirror check). The benchmark itself ran 3 times on a real Android emulator (emulator-5554) -- all three passed, all three showed the same qualitative pattern. Real bundle rebuilt via dart run flutter_pear:pack after adding the debug.echo handler.

REVIEW: touches pear-end/schema.dart (a new debug-only method) but the change itself is small and low-risk (a stateless identity echo, no lifecycle/data-structure complexity) -- ran one focused review agent rather than a full multi-angle workflow, proportionate to the risk. Found one real, worth-fixing issue: the benchmark's own assertions only checked payload LENGTH, not byte-for-byte content, which wouldn't have caught a transport corrupting bytes while preserving size (the exact historical bug class E4.4's frame-coalescing issue was). Fixed: both benchmark helpers now assert full byte-for-byte equality against the original random payload. Confirmed safe: debug.echo's malformed-input behavior (falls through to the same uncoded-err convention every other handler already uses, no crash), the dual-PearRpc-per-worklet pattern (verified against the real rpc.dart/bare_worklet.dart source, not just plausible-sounding), and BENCHMARK.md's arithmetic. Re-ran the benchmark on-device after the assertion fix -- still green, same pattern (16MB: file-path ~2.6x faster).

DEFERRED (per this project's standing "automated tests first, hardware last" decision, same treatment as E1.5/E5.2/E5.3): this ticket's STEPS also call for running the benchmark "on one physical device + one emulator" -- the physical-device leg is deferred and tracked in flutter_pear-doi; the emulator leg (this closing record) is real, reproducible evidence, not a placeholder. The committed harness is directly reusable for that pass whenever it runs.
close_reason: E5.1 done: real, reproducible on-device (emulator) benchmark confirms the LOCKED file-path default for bulk transport -- file-path is ~2-2.5x faster than in-channel at 1MB/16MB, consistently across 3 runs (see packages/flutter_pear/BENCHMARK.md for the full table + PearDrive/E5.5 recommendation). Harness committed at packages/flutter_pear_example/integration_test/bulk_transport_benchmark_test.dart, reusable for the deferred physical-device pass (tracked in flutter_pear-doi). New debug.echo RPC method (debug-only, mirrors debugForceError/debugForceCrash's precedent) reviewed and found safe; one real gap in the benchmark's own assertions (length-only, not content) found and fixed. melos analyze/test green throughout (84/84 flutter_pear). E5.5 (PearDrive) is now unblocked.

title: E4.5: Release-mode packaging verification (R8, app bundle, splits)
description:
CONTEXT: E4 (codex #9 fold). Debug builds mask release breakage: R8/ProGuard stripping, app-bundle/split-APK packaging of native libs.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/ (consumer proguard rules file if needed), packages/flutter_pear_example/android/.
STEPS:
1. Build the example as an app bundle (flutter build appbundle --release) and as split APKs; install the device-specific split on hardware.
2. Exercise chat end-to-end on the release install; watch for R8-stripped reflection/JNI symbols — add consumer-rules.pro entries to the LIBRARY (so app devs inherit them) for anything stripped.
3. Record the verified packaging modes in a comment/README dev note.
VALIDATION: release app-bundle install runs two-device chat; consumer rules (if any) live in the library, not the example.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Release app-bundle + split-APK installs run chat on hardware; any required keep rules ship as library consumer rules; verified modes documented.
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (6 numbered checks) found zero P1s but one real, legitimate CONCERN in my FIRST draft of the fix -- exactly described above (Build.SUPPORTED_ABIS checks device capability, not the installed package's actual native libs, so it wouldn't catch a wrong-split-on-a-capable-device scenario despite being described that way). Fixed immediately (not deferred) by redesigning around the actual native-load failure via try/catch(LinkageError) instead of a predictive heuristic, and verified on-device via dumpsys package that the ALTERNATIVE heuristic I considered (a nativeLibraryDir file check) would have been WORSE (a guaranteed false positive under extractNativeLibs=false). Reviewer also confirmed: the guard is correctly placed after the hot-restart-reattach early-return (doesn't redundantly re-check a live worklet), the exception type is correctly caught by the existing onMethodCall try/catch and surfaces as a readable Dart PlatformException, and the suggested alternative fix (an example-app-level splits{abi{}} Gradle block) is moot since flutter_pear_example/android/ is entirely untracked/regenerated (flutter create hydration), not a place to durably encode project-wide config.
close_reason: Release app-bundle (.aab) and split-APK (--split-per-abi) builds both verified working, including a real on-device release-mode boot (R8 active) with zero crash. Found and fixed a genuine packaging gap along the way: the armeabi-v7a variant both artifact types produce by default is missing this plugin's native libraries entirely (32-bit is deliberately unsupported) -- after two rejected approaches (a library-level ndk.abiFilters, confirmed to have no effect on a consuming app's splits; a device-capability precheck, correctly flagged by review as not actually testing what's installed), landed on reacting to the real native-load failure via try/catch(LinkageError) with a clear, actionable message instead of a cryptic native-loader crash. Verified via dumpsys package that a plausible alternative (a native-library-directory file check) would have been worse -- a guaranteed false positive under the modern extractNativeLibs=false packaging mode. Documented in README.md; no new consumer-rules.pro entries needed. Two-device hardware chat validation remains deferred per standing project directive.

title: E4.2: Fetch UX — progress logs, retry-once, manual fallback, cache docs
description:
CONTEXT: E4, DX X3 (locked D7 + codex #1/#14). The first build downloads native binaries for minutes inside 'Running Gradle task assembleDebug...' — indistinguishable from a hang. The persona's tolerance is ONE failed build.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle (fetch task from E4.1), README install section note (one line — full README pass is epic E8).
STEPS:
1. Fetch task logs "Downloading Bare Kit vX (N MB)..." + completion, visible in flutter run -v.
2. Failed/corrupt download: retry ONCE, then fail with the artifact URL + a documented manual-download fallback path (drop file here, build continues).
3. Document cache location + how to clear it (comment in build.gradle + line for the README).
VALIDATION: simulate failure (block the URL via hosts or bogus URL override) -> one retry -> actionable error naming URL + fallback; happy path shows progress lines in -v output.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Progress visible in flutter run -v; failure retries once then errors with URL + manual fallback instructions; cache location documented; simulated-failure run demonstrates the full path.
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (8 numbered checks) found zero P1s. One low-severity CONCERN (no log line when the URL override is active, risking a silently-stale override in a shared gradle.properties) was fixed immediately before close, rather than filed as a follow-up, since it was a one-line addition -- re-validated afterward that the new warning line prints correctly alongside the existing downloading/failure messages. Reviewer independently confirmed on disk that the documented cache path (build/flutter_pear_bare/bare-kit/2.3.0/prebuilds.zip, 371197422 bytes, matching the pinned sha256 exactly) is accurate, and traced the retry loop's partFile.delete()-before-retry logic plus Ant's default overwrite-on-dest behavior to confirm no stale-partial-file corruption risk across retries or across separate task invocations.
close_reason: fetchBareKit's silent download replaced with progress logging (real content-length via HEAD request), a retry-once loop, and an actionable failure message naming the exact URL and manual-fallback path -- plus corrected cache-location documentation (build/flutter_pear_bare/bare-kit/<version>/, verified on disk, not the naively-assumed packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/build/) in both a build.gradle comment and a new README line. Reviewed independently with zero P1s (one minor concern fixed inline). Validated via real simulated-failure and real HEAD-request checks against the actual GitHub release, plus a full clean app build.

title: E3.4: CI job running the full Dart suite against the fake
description:
CONTEXT: E3. Make the fake the default CI substrate so every push exercises the full Dart stack with zero devices.
FILES: .github/workflows/ci.yml (new or extend), melos.yaml test script.
STEPS:
1. GitHub Actions job: melos bootstrap -> melos run analyze -> melos run test on ubuntu-latest (no emulator).
2. Ensure flutter_pear's fake-backed integration tests run in that job.
3. Badge in README optional; keep the job under ~5 min.
VALIDATION: push a branch, job green; intentionally break a spine test locally to confirm the job would catch it (then revert).

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Every push runs bootstrap+analyze+test with fake-backed integration coverage on a hosted runner, no devices, <~5 min; verified to fail on a broken spine test.
notes: Honest scope note: 'push a branch, job green' (the ticket's own VALIDATION wording) could NOT be literally performed -- this repo's standing policy is manual commit/push only (developer does it by hand), so an actual live GitHub Actions execution was not triggered or observed. Everything possible to verify WITHOUT pushing was verified: the exact command sequence the workflow runs, confirmed to pass AND correctly fail on a broken test locally; the YAML's syntax validated; the workflow's structure (checkout + subosito/flutter-action, a standard well-established action) matches common, reliable patterns. The developer pushing this branch (or a PR) will show the FIRST real run; if anything about the hosted-runner environment specifically (SDK download timing, apt-level differences from this dev machine) surfaces an issue, it isn't something local validation could have caught either way.
close_reason: First CI infrastructure in this repo: .github/workflows/ci.yml runs melos bootstrap/analyze/test on ubuntu-latest with zero devices or Node.js setup (pack_test.dart already gracefully skips when bare-pack/npm are absent). Found and fixed a real melos interactive-prompt bug along the way (--no-select), applied consistently to the CI job and to CLAUDE.md/README.md's documented local dev command. Verified the exact command sequence passes locally and correctly fails (exit 1, right test named) when a spine test is intentionally broken, then cleanly reverted. Actual live GitHub Actions execution couldn't be triggered under the repo's manual-push-only policy -- honestly noted as the one thing only a real push can confirm.

title: E3.3: Failure-injection hooks (crash/timeout/stale-nonce/drop/blocked)
description:
CONTEXT: E3. Failure injection is why the fake earns its keep: every spine failure path must be drivable in a unit test.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear_test/lib/src/failure_injection.dart (+ hooks on FakeBareWorklet), tests.
STEPS: expose test hooks to simulate — worklet crash (fires the control-channel crash path -> onCrash), call timeout (swallow a request), stale-nonce frame (send with wrong session), 0x01/unknown discriminator frame, connection drop mid-stream, UDP-blocked network (state -> failed with E_UDP_BLOCKED after the bound).
Each hook mirrors the REAL wire behavior defined by the schema — no fake-only shortcuts.
VALIDATION: one test per hook in flutter_pear_test; flutter_pear spine tests (E2.2-E2.7) each have a fake-driven variant proving app-visible behavior (typed exceptions, onCrash, failed state).

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
