6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: All six injection hooks exist, mirror real wire behavior, and each drives a green test demonstrating the app-visible outcome (typed error, onCrash, failed state).
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (8 numbered checks, ran the actual test suites and traced the part-of privacy mechanism, the nonce-gate rejection path, and idempotency guards independently) found zero P1s. Two P2-level concerns were fixed before close rather than just noted: (1) only 2 of 6 hooks (E2.6, E2.7) initially had a fake-driven variant literally living in flutter_pear's own spine test files -- fixed by adding the remaining 4 (E2.2-E2.5) to rpc_test.dart, now covering all six spine tickets' worth of behavior in the exact files VALIDATION names, not just in flutter_pear_test's own suite. (2) the disconnectFrom test only exercised an idle connection, not a literal mid-stream drop -- fixed by having data actively flow through the connection (a real write + received assertion) immediately before triggering the drop, so the close event is proven to interrupt an active exchange, not just tear down an unused connection.
close_reason: All six failure-injection hooks implemented on FakeBareWorklet (crash, timeout, stale-nonce, unknown-discriminator, connection-drop via existing disconnectFrom, and UDP-blocked failure), each mirroring real wire behavior with no fake-only shortcuts. 20 new tests total across both packages (7 in flutter_pear_test proving each hook's mechanism via real PearRpc/PearSwarm; 6 fake-driven variants added to flutter_pear's own rpc_test.dart/swarm_test.dart covering all of E2.2 through E2.7 literally in those files, per VALIDATION's explicit ask). Reviewed independently with zero P1s; two P2 gaps found by review (incomplete per-ticket variant coverage, an idle rather than literal mid-stream connection-drop test) were both fixed before close, not deferred.

title: E3.2: In-memory fake swarm/worklet implementing the schema
description:
CONTEXT: E3. The fake implements the schema surface PearSwarm/PearConnection consume — a CONFORMANCE CONSUMER (locked: schema wins; the fake never defines behavior). Deterministic: no real timers/radios; two fakes in one process can talk.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear_test/lib/src/fake_worklet.dart (implements the same seam BareWorklet exposes: incoming stream + send), fake_swarm_backend.dart, tests.
STEPS:
1. Implement a FakeBareWorklet honoring the wire schema: attach handshake (nonce+version), request/response for swarm.join/leave + connection.write, events swarm.connection/connection.data/connection.close, discriminator byte, X8 state events.
2. In-memory hub: two FakeBareWorklets joined to the same topic exchange connection events + data synchronously/microtask-deterministically.
3. Prove conformance by construction: PearRpc + PearSwarm from flutter_pear run UNMODIFIED against the fake.
4. Tests: two fake peers join same topic -> both get connections; write on A -> data on B; leave closes B's connection stream; state stream reaches connected.
VALIDATION: cd packages/flutter_pear_test && flutter test green; a test in flutter_pear's own suite runs PearSwarm end-to-end against the fake.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: PearSwarm/PearRpc run unmodified against FakeBareWorklet; two fake peers exchange join/data/close + state events deterministically; covered by tests in both packages.
notes: Review process: independent Agent-tool review (8 numbered checks) ran the actual test suites, wrote and ran its OWN throwaway test to independently reproduce the documented race-condition claim (confirmed accurate, not overstated), verified the pubspec dev-dependency-cycle safety claim via dart pub deps, and empirically verified the flutter-analyze-flags-path-deps claim in a scratch copy -- found zero P1s. One CONCERN (disconnectFrom, the fake's only connection.close-emitting path, was unused/untested) was fixed immediately before close (see test evidence above) rather than deferred, since STEP 1 explicitly required connection.close coverage. Reviewer also confirmed the multi-topic connection bookkeeping (_connectionTopics) correctly mirrors pear-end/index.js's real E4.4 per-topic fan-out behavior (not speculative complexity) despite having no dedicated multi-topic test of its own -- accepted as justified-but-untested, matching the real production code's own design.
close_reason: FakeBareWorklet + FakeSwarmHub implemented as a true conformance consumer of the RPC schema -- PearSwarm/PearConnection run completely unmodified against it, proven by a dedicated cross-package conformance test in flutter_pear's own suite. Found and fixed a real event-ordering race during implementation (ack-before-connect-event), with the residual, narrower fragility clearly documented rather than papered over. 7 new tests total (6 in flutter_pear_test, 1 in flutter_pear), all green; reviewed independently with zero P1s. One review-flagged gap (connection.close wire coverage) fixed inline before close. A forward-looking PearSwarm API question surfaced by this work is tracked separately in flutter_pear-4w6, correctly out of scope here.

title: E3.1: Scaffold flutter_pear_test package
description:
CONTEXT: E3. New publishable package flutter_pear_test — pub.dev rules apply from day one (LICENSE per package, CHANGELOG, README, dartdoc).
FILES: new packages/flutter_pear_test/{pubspec.yaml,LICENSE,CHANGELOG.md,README.md,lib/flutter_pear_test.dart}, melos.yaml membership check.
STEPS:
1. Scaffold the package (MIT LICENSE copy, 0.0.1 CHANGELOG, README stating purpose: in-memory fakes so app devs unit-test P2P without radios/peers; fake conforms to schema — schema wins on any disagreement).
2. Depend on flutter_pear (path within monorepo) for the schema constants; no protocol deps.
3. Empty-but-documented lib entrypoint exporting nothing yet.
VALIDATION: melos bootstrap links it; melos run analyze includes it and passes; dart pub publish --dry-run reports only the path-dependency issue (expected pre-publish), nothing else.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Package exists with LICENSE/CHANGELOG/README/dartdoc conventions, links via melos, analyze passes, publish dry-run clean except expected path deps.
notes: Test evidence: melos bootstrap links the new package (4 packages bootstrapped, up from 3). melos run analyze -- zero issues across all 4 packages. dart pub publish --dry-run reports only the expected path-dependency error + the monorepo-wide pubspec_overrides.yaml hints (verified equivalent to flutter_pear's own dry-run output). flutter test in flutter_pear (42) and flutter_pear_bare (6) -- unaffected, still green. No test/ dir added to flutter_pear_test itself -- there's no logic yet to test (pure scaffolding; the fake implementation and its tests are E3.2's job, already correctly declared as the child this ticket blocks).
close_reason: flutter_pear_test scaffolded with LICENSE/CHANGELOG/README/analysis_options matching sibling package conventions, an empty-but-documented lib entrypoint, and a path dependency on flutter_pear for schema constants. Links via melos bootstrap, analyzes clean, and dart pub publish --dry-run reports only the expected pre-publish path-dependency issue (verified equivalent to every sibling package's own dry-run output, not a new problem). Trivial scaffolding diff -- no independent review needed.

title: E9: CI/CD + publishing — reproducible bundle, license enforcement, pub.dev v0.1 (Android)
description:
GOAL: Reproducible builds, enforced licensing, and the pub.dev release train. Publish v0.1 (ANDROID-ONLY — locked; iOS is v0.2 with its own podspec/Xcode/background milestone) once the full stack + lifecycle + license assets are wired. Single release, no preview slices (user's settled office-hours decision — do not relitigate).

WORK ITEMS:
1. T9 reproducible bundle (codex #11): pin pear-end JS deps (drop caret ranges in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/package.json); STOP .gitignoring lockfiles — commit package-lock.json so the shipped bundle is reproducible.
2. T10 license collector enforcement (codex #12): packages/flutter_pear/bin/pack.dart currently SILENTLY SKIPS modules without license files. Make it: FAIL (not skip) on missing license; reject forbidden SPDX values (GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL/unlicensed); walk nested dependency trees; include native Bare Kit notices; regenerate NOTICE without stale output. Extend packages/flutter_pear/test/pack_test.dart for each behavior.
3. T11 publishing mechanics (codex #10): flutter_pear has a PATH dependency on flutter_pear_bare (packages/flutter_pear/pubspec.yaml:14) — pub.dev requires separately published packages with hosted version constraints. Define publication order (flutter_pear_bare -> flutter_pear_test -> flutter_pear) and convert path deps at publish time. Publish-on-tag workflow.
4. Compatibility table as CI contract (codex #13 + eng): plugin <-> Bare Kit <-> Hyper* versions PLUS the tested toolchain row (Flutter/AGP/Gradle/Kotlin/JDK/NDK/minSdk) — one tested contract, checked in CI against pinned versions.
5. X10 device matrix CI: arm64 device + x86_64 emulator jobs; RELEASE-mode job (R8/app-bundle) so debug never masks release breakage (codex #9).
6. X9 TTHW enforcement (locked, DX D12): the fresh-machine CI job (create app -> add package -> build -> run) gets timestamps and FAILS if clone->running exceeds the 5-minute budget. TTHW regression = red CI forever. Plus quarterly /devex-review boomerang on the live package (calendar habit — CI can't feel device auth, camera prompts, cold caches).
7. Encryption criterion test (codex #13-eng): capture transport bytes between two peers; assert an authenticated Noise session + decryption only by the intended peer ("not plaintext" alone can pass on framing/encoding).
8. GitHub Discussions + issue templates ON at publish day (TODOS.md item; templates per project_plan.md section 6 — include flutter doctor + worklet log).

ACCEPTANCE: tag -> CI green across matrix (debug+release, device+emulator, fresh-machine under 5 min, licenses enforced, compat table verified, snippets compile) -> packages land on pub.dev in order -> flutter pub add flutter_pear on a clean machine builds and runs the chat demo. pub.dev score >= 130.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Distribution Plan, T9-T11, X9/X10, Success Criteria)
acceptance_criteria: Every quality gate passes when run BY HAND (this repo has no CI): melos run analyze; melos run test; pear-end npm test; check_compatibility.dart; extract_snippets.dart; pana score >= 130 for flutter_pear_bare (flutter_pear/flutter_pear_test pana score is only meaningful post-publish -- see df9 notes); pack.dart license enforcement (fails on missing/forbidden SPDX); Noise confidentiality test; device+emulator per-package integration tests (STEP 5 physical coverage remains flutter_pear-doi, per project_plan.md). Then: ordered manual publish to pub.dev (flutter_pear_bare -> flutter_pear -> flutter_pear_test); flutter pub add flutter_pear builds and runs chat on a clean machine.
notes:
Reminder from flutter_pear-5rl: when this epic sets up a device/emulator CI job (likely E9.3), also wire in flutter_pear_example/integration_test/ipc_transport_test.dart (a real on-device attach.info round-trip test guarding against the E1.3-E2.5 BinaryCodec-class transport bug recurring silently) -- run via 'flutter test integration_test/ipc_transport_test.dart -d <device>'.
E9.6 Noise confidentiality proof CLOSED (see flutter_pear-df9.6 for full detail): packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/test/noise-confidentiality.test.js, 6 real-crypto tests against a local hyperdht testnet. Confirms: transport bytes never contain a fresh high-entropy marker (confidentiality), a raw unencrypted socket control does contain it (proves the capture methodology works), both sides authenticate the correct remote public key both via the raw library and via pear-end/index.js own worklet event contract, and three wrong-peer-rejection angles including a literal decrypt-with-wrong-key attempt against real sodium-secretstream internals (not just inferred from ciphertext opacity). All 32 pear-end tests and 203 flutter_pear Dart tests green.
DECISION (2026-07-05, explicit user instruction): remove github workflow, do not use it. Deleted .github/workflows/ci.yml, fresh-machine.yml, and publish.yml entirely -- this repo no longer uses GitHub Actions/CI in any form. Context: while investigating a real failed-CI notification, found three genuine problems in the pushed workflows (missing melos bootstrap before dependency resolution in 4 jobs; the E9.7 path-to-hosted pubspec flip creating a pre-publish chicken-and-egg that breaks both the pana score gate and the fresh-machine external-consumer smoke; and GitHub-hosted macos-latest emulator runners failing to boot with HV_UNSUPPORTED, i.e. no reliable nested-virtualization for the Android emulator). Rather than keep fixing CI infra, the user opted to drop GitHub Actions entirely. IMPLICATION: every child ticket whose ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA assumes a CI job (df9.3, df9.4, df9.5, df9.6, df9.7, df9.8, ph3.2, ph3.7, 0ux.4) now needs re-reading against this reality -- their underlying tools (melos test/analyze, check_compatibility.dart, extract_snippets.dart, pana, pack.dart license enforcement) all still work fine standalone and should be run by hand before every push; only the auto-run-on-push/PR automation layer is gone. Doc references to the deleted workflow files have been updated (COMPATIBILITY.md, flutter_pear/pubspec.yaml, flutter_pear_bare/pubspec.yaml, tool/extract_snippets.dart) to describe manual invocation instead of CI enforcement. Not yet re-scoped: the epic ACCEPTANCE line itself ("tag -> CI green across matrix... -> pub.dev") and df9.3/df9.8 explicitly, which still reference CI green as a gate -- left for the user to decide how (or whether) to replace that with a manual checklist.
CONFIRMED VIA REAL RUN (2026-07-05, not just log analysis): flutter_pear is currently NOT installable by any external consumer outside this exact monorepo -- flutter pub add flutter_pear --path <checkout> fails immediately with a pub version-solve error because flutter_pear depends on flutter_pear_bare as a hosted (^0.0.1) constraint that does not exist on pub.dev yet (pre-publish). This directly violates CLAUDE.mds own non-negotiable "one-line install" convention and blocks df9.3 (TTHW gate) from ever getting a real number pre-publish. Root cause is the E9.7/E9.8 publish-day sequencing gap: E9.7 (closed) already flipped path deps to hosted constraints in anticipation of a REAL publish, but E9.8 (the actual pub.dev publish) has not happened yet, so the hosted constraint currently points at nothing. THREE OPTIONS presented to the user (none implemented -- this needs a human decision, not a unilateral fix, since it either reverts already-closed E9.7 work or takes the irreversible step of actually publishing to pub.dev): (1) revert flutter_pear_bare to a path dependency until the actual publish day, re-flipping to hosted only immediately before running dart pub publish for real; (2) go ahead and actually publish flutter_pear_bare to pub.dev now (a real, external, hard-to-reverse action); (3) accept "one-line install currently broken" as a documented, deliberate pre-publish gap and defer both df9.3 and this convention check until publish day. Flagged, not resolved.
RESOLVED (2026-07-06) per developer decision (option 1 of 3 presented): reverted flutter_pear/pubspec.yaml and flutter_pear_test/pubspec.yaml back to path: dependencies for their sibling packages (flutter_pear_bare, and for flutter_pear_test: both flutter_pear and flutter_pear_bare), restoring publish_to: none on both (removed by E9.7, needed again now that path deps make flutter analyze correctly flag them as not publish-ready). melos bootstrap re-run, all lockfiles/overrides regenerated. REAL RE-VERIFICATION: tool/fresh_machine_check.sh now genuinely PASSES against a real emulator -- worklet attached in 61s (budget 300s), one-line install (flutter pub add flutter_pear --path <checkout>) confirmed working again from a fresh consumer app outside the melos workspace. pana scores also recovered: flutter_pear 150/160 (was 50/160), flutter_pear_test 160/160 (was 50/160), flutter_pear_bare unaffected at 160/160 -- all comfortably clear the 130 gate. Full suites re-confirmed green: melos run analyze (0 issues), melos run test (204 Dart tests). Also ran dart format on packages/flutter_pear (7 files had pre-existing formatting drift, unrelated to this fix) to clear a pana static-analysis deduction. REMAINING: re-flip flutter_pear_bare/flutter_pear/flutter_pear_test back to hosted ^0.0.1 constraints (and re-remove publish_to: none on the latter two) in the same step as the real E9.8 dart pub publish -- not before. This repos own comments at each edit site document this explicitly.
ALL 11 CHILDREN CLOSED, EPIC ACCEPTANCE FULLY MET (2026-07-06). Final state: flutter_pear_bare/flutter_pear/flutter_pear_test all published to pub.dev at 0.0.1, in the correct dependency order, hosted constraints kept permanently (this is the real end-state E9.7 always intended -- the interim path-dependency revert was only for the pre-publish gap, now resolved for real). git tag v0.0.1 pushed matching the published commit. All quality gates re-confirmed passing by hand: melos run analyze/test, pear-end npm test (34 tests), check_compatibility.dart, pana (flutter_pear ~150-160/160, flutter_pear_bare 160/160, flutter_pear_test 160/160, all well clear of 130), Noise confidentiality test, all device+emulator integration suites (flutter_pear-g28). Post-publish smoke test definitively passed: a genuinely fresh external app, real hosted flutter pub add flutter_pear, real two-way chat against a desktop peer on a real Android emulator over the real public Hyperswarm DHT. GitHub Discussions + issue templates live (flutter_pear-df9.8). Doc-freshness pass completed across README.md x4, CLAUDE.md, COMPATIBILITY.md, project_plan.md. v0.1 (Android) is genuinely shipped.
close_reason: All 11 children closed, every acceptance criterion met with real evidence: published to pub.dev (all 3 packages, correct order), git-tagged, all quality gates hand-verified, post-publish smoke test definitively passed. v0.1 (Android) shipped for real.

title: E8: Docs + error DX — README truth pass, error catalog, unsafeTopicFromString
description:
GOAL: Docs and errors that match the persona's tolerance (Flutter app dev, ~15 min / ONE failed build, won't read protocol docs, copies from README). Errors follow the formula: what happened + why + how to fix + where to learn more + actual values.

WORK ITEMS:
1. X2 README truth pass (locked, DX D6 + codex #15/#16): per-API status column on the coverage table (works today / M2 / M3...); a "Works today" section ABOVE the target-API quick start; chat-first flow (advanced storage concepts kept OUT of the initial README path); pub.dev above-the-fold: Android-only warning, prerequisites, exact copy-paste example, expected output, troubleshooting link; pre-1.0 pin note ("minor versions may break; pin your version"). CI job compiles README snippets against the real API so drift cannot recur (the swarm.leave(topic) vs leave() drift ALREADY happened at 95 lines of code — packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/swarm.dart:81 vs README quick start).
2. X4 error catalog (locked, DX D8 + codex #12): every err.code maps to problem + likely cause + concrete fix + stable docs anchor (e.g. #E_UDP_BLOCKED). JS stack demoted to a details field — visible, never leading. Catalog is a checked-in table that BOTH pear-end and Dart reference (single source). Coverage includes the X8 connection-failure codes AND a troubleshooting page for install-time failure classes (Gradle fetch, NDK, ABI mismatch, manifest merge) — the dominant first-install failures that runtime err.codes can't catch.
3. X6 rename (locked, DX D10): PearCrypto.topicFromString -> unsafeTopicFromString (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/crypto.dart). The global-collision footgun (every end user worldwide with the same string lands in ONE room, silently) must be carried by the NAME — docs can be skipped, names can't. Quick start keeps it for the 2-device demo with a one-line steer: real apps use PearPairing invites.
4. Docs IA per project_plan.md section 6 (mirrors Pear's own docs): Quick start -> Concepts (worklet model, keys/topics vs invites, replication) -> How-tos (chat, file sync, pairing) -> API reference (dartdoc) -> Troubleshooting (NAT/relay behavior, background limits, common build errors). Concepts leads with topics-vs-invites.
5. dartdoc on every public symbol across all packages (pub.dev score target >= 130 enforces this).

ACCEPTANCE: snippet-compile CI red on any README/API drift. A forced worklet error renders all four fields (problem/cause/fix/anchor). unsafeTopicFromString is the only public spelling. The persona can answer "what works TODAY?" from the README's first screen.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (DX X2/X4/X6, First-Time Developer Confusion Report, journey map)
acceptance_criteria: Snippet-compile CI red on README/API drift; status matrix + Works-today section above target API; every err.code renders problem+cause+fix+doc anchor with JS stack demoted to details; unsafeTopicFromString is the only public spelling; dartdoc on every public symbol.
close_reason: All 8 children closed; acceptance criteria met: snippet-compile CI (README+docs), status matrix/works-today README section, error catalog with anchors, unsafeTopicFromString the only public spelling, 100% dartdoc coverage (pana >=130 all 3 packages)

title: E7: Example app DX — QR-pairing chat, desktop CLI peer, doctor tool
description:
GOAL: The example app is the product demo, the magical-moment vehicle, and the TTHW proof. Committed target (locked, DX D3): CHAMPION tier — clone -> two-peer chat in UNDER 5 MINUTES. The magical moment: a message appears on peer B and the developer realizes there is no server anywhere.

WORK ITEMS:
1. X1 QR-pairing chat example (locked, DX D4): commit the example runners (android/ hydrated and CHECKED IN — the "hydrate once with flutter create" step is gone). Phone A taps "Start room" -> shows QR of topic/invite; phone B scans -> chat connects. One flutter run per device, zero key typing. Camera-permission denial recovery + manual short-code entry fallback (codex #4). The QR scan doubles as the blind-pairing demo once PearPairing lands; before that it may encode a topic. Multi-device flutter run ergonomics documented (-d flags, two terminals).
2. X7 desktop CLI peer (locked, DX D11): dart run flutter_pear_example:peer joins a topic from the dev's laptop as the second peer — Bare runs on desktop. Solves the one-phone developer (a big slice of the persona: emulators sit behind NAT that can break UDP hole-punching). Doubles as the scriptable second peer for CI integration tests.
3. X5 doctor tool (user decision, DX D9): dart run flutter_pear:doctor — connectivity check, DHT reachability, NAT type; names the blocker on a UDP-blocked network. --report prints a telemetry-free support bundle (plugin/native/bundle versions, ABI, Android version, sanitized recent log) for pasting into GitHub issues (codex #18). Scope note: doctor is a RUNTIME diagnostic; install-time failures (Gradle/NDK) are the error catalog + troubleshooting docs' job (codex #11).
4. Emulator/device pairing combos verified and documented: device<->device, device<->emulator, emulator<->emulator, device<->desktop-peer — README states exactly which work.
5. README GIF: 15-second capture of the QR->chat flow (passive preview that converts browsers into cloners).

ACCEPTANCE: stopwatch run on a fresh machine: clone -> melos bootstrap -> flutter run x2 -> QR scan -> message on both screens in under 5 minutes. One-phone path: phone + laptop CLI peer reaches the same moment. Doctor on a hostile network names the blocker instead of shrugging.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (DX Review — Magical Moment Specification, X1/X5/X7, Competitive DX Benchmark)
acceptance_criteria: Stopwatch: clone -> two-peer chat under 5 minutes (champion TTHW); one-phone path works via desktop CLI peer; camera-denial + manual-code fallback works; doctor names the blocker on a UDP-blocked network and --report prints a telemetry-free support bundle; pairing combos documented.
notes:
STOPWATCH EVIDENCE (E7.6 VALIDATION, emulator-based per this session's hardware-downgrade decision): timed a genuine fresh 'git clone' (SSH, real GitHub remote, not a local path) through to a confirmed two-way chat message delivery.

Steps timed (wall clock, this session, warm Gradle/pub/npm global caches -- cold-cache first run would be slower, mostly Gradle's first-ever daemon/dependency download):
- git clone (SSH) -> repo on disk: instant
- melos bootstrap (pub get x4 packages): 18s (cumulative 18s)
- npm install in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end: 1s (cumulative 19s)
- flutter build apk --debug --target-platform android-arm64 (Gradle assembleDebug): 119.8s (cumulative ~139s)
- adb install + am start on a running emulator: ~1s (cumulative ~140s)
- dart run flutter_pear_example:peer --topic stopwatchclone (build-hook compile + Hyperswarm join): ~13s (cumulative ~153s)
- phone: tap Chat demo -> type room name -> tap Join: ~11s (cumulative ~164s)
- Hyperswarm DHT discovery + Noise handshake to connected: 14s (cumulative ~178s)
- type + send message on phone, confirmed received on desktop peer (peer: stopwatch): 12s (cumulative 260s)

TOTAL: 260s (4m20s) clone -> confirmed working two-way chat. Under the 5-minute target in u8y.6's VALIDATION line.

Caveat: emulator-based per the developer's explicit 'downgrade acceptance criteria to emulator-only' decision this session (flutter_pear-vyw.5) -- no physical Android hardware available in this dev environment. Uses the emulator<->desktop-peer combo (the one confirmed reliable), not emulator<->emulator (confirmed broken, NAT artifact, documented in README).
close_reason: All 7 children closed. Epic acceptance satisfied via the one-phone path explicitly documented in its own ACCEPTANCE clause ('phone + laptop CLI peer reaches the same moment'): timed clone->melos bootstrap->build->install->join room->two-way chat = 260s (4m20s), under 5 minutes, evidence on this epic. QR-scan two-phone path (X1) is implemented and unit-tested (u8y.2) but its own stopwatch run needs two physical devices/cameras, deferred to flutter_pear-doi's hardware-validation pass per this session's developer decision to downgrade acceptance criteria to emulator-only where physical hardware isn't available. Doctor tool (u8y.4/u8y.5), desktop peer (u8y.3), file-drop demo (u8y.7), and the pairing-combo matrix + GIF (u8y.6) are all done and closed.

title: E5: Data-structure wrappers — Corestore/Hypercore → Hyperbee → Hyperdrive → pairing → Autobase-last
description:
GOAL: Fan out the typed Dart wrappers for the full Pear stack against the locked schema, in DEPENDENCY ORDER (locked — the "any order" premise was wrong): Corestore + Hypercore are the substrate everything sits on and come FIRST; then Hyperbee; then Hyperdrive (streaming risk); then blind-pairing; AUTOBASE LAST (multi-writer risk).

METHOD (locked): hand-write the first two wrappers (PearStore/PearCore against Corestore/Hypercore, then PearBee) directly against the schema, green on the flutter_pear_test fake the day they're written. Decide codegen ONLY if the third wrapper's boilerplate is provably mechanical and repetitive. Every wrapper: expose the JS module over RPC in pear-end, add the typed Dart wrapper — never reimplement any protocol in Dart.

PER-WRAPPER CHECKLIST (applies to every package below):
- pear-end: expose the module over the schema (request/response + events, typed err.codes registered in the error catalog)
- Dart: typed wrapper — Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, Uint8List for binary, PearKey for keys, dispose() per Flutter norms, dartdoc on every public symbol
- fake: extend flutter_pear_test in lockstep
- tests: unit tests against the fake (happy + error paths) AND at least one real-device integration test against the real worklet (guards fake/real drift — codex #9)
- licensing: before adding ANY pear-end dependency confirm MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC; reject GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL/unlicensed; regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES via dart run flutter_pear:pack

ORDER + RISK NOTES:
1. PearStore/PearCore (Corestore/Hypercore): substrate. Storage paths per platform. err.codes -> PearStorageException.
2. PearBee (Hyperbee): get/put/del, range streams, watch. Range streams = first real use of event-stream subscribe/unsubscribe.
3. PearDrive (Hyperdrive): BULK RISK. Locked transport decision: file-path import/export (worklet writes to disk, Dart reads the file) or pull-based bounded reads — NOT in-channel streaming unless the throughput benchmark demands it. A bulk transfer large enough to require chunking must complete without exhausting memory.
4. PearPairing (blind-pairing): invites, device linking. Feeds the example app's QR flow.
5. PearBase (Autobase) LAST — HARD. Locked decision (codex #2): do NOT expose generic open/apply (incompatible with zero-JS; Dart callbacks across RPC during deterministic indexing are slow/reentrant/failure-prone). Ship PREBUILT MERGE RECIPES in the pear-end (last-writer-wins, ordered-log, CRDT-map); Dart selects by name. Generic apply = documented custom-worklet advanced path only.

PINNED OPEN QUESTIONS (resolve during, not before): reconnect/delivery semantics after network switch (M1); topic/key persistence, backup & device migration (M2).

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (P2 caveat, Next Steps 3b/4, T12) and project_plan.md section 4 (API coverage map)
acceptance_criteria: Each wrapper: typed Dart API green on the fake day-one + >=1 real-device integration test; Hyperdrive bulk via file-path import/export completes a chunk-scale transfer without exhausting memory; Autobase ships prebuilt recipes (LWW/ordered-log/CRDT-map) selectable by name, NO generic apply in the default path; all pear-end deps license-checked.
notes:
E5.4b CODEGEN RE-EVALUATION (after wrapper 3=PearDrive/Hyperdrive closed, per E5.4's own deferred checkpoint): NO-GO, and this time conclusively -- not "revisit later."

QUANTIFIED EVIDENCE WITH 3 SAMPLES (vs E5.4's 2): store.dart 119 lines, bee.dart 202, drive.dart 121 (442 total). Tests: store_test 268, bee_test 342, drive_test 266 (876 total). pear-end handler-case sections: E5.2 ~80 lines, E5.3 ~124, E5.5 ~146 (growing, not shrinking, as each wrapper's real underlying JS library -- Hypercore's log, Hyperbee's tree, Hyperdrive's dual-core file/blob split -- adds its own genuinely novel translation work). Mechanical fraction still measures ~35-40% (open validation, reopen-after-close guard, error wrapping, replicate/close idempotency, fake writer+merge+replicate-handshake, schema doc shape, several near-identical test cases) -- consistent with E5.4's 2-sample estimate, now confirmed with a 3rd, architecturally distinct wrapper (file/blob storage, not another log or tree).

NEW, DECISIVE FINDING this 3rd sample surfaces: the "mechanical" reopen-after-close pattern is NOT actually template-safe even within its own established shape -- E5.5's review caught DRIVE_OPEN getting a SUBTLY WORSE version of a bug E5.2's STORE_GET already had (a discarded session leaking on redundant reopen; E5.5's version compounded it by also constructing a full second Hyperdrive with 2 more sessions before the registration guard). A follow-up (flutter_pear-0md) now tracks retrofitting E5.2/E5.3 for the SAME lesson E5.5 only learned by being reviewed. This means even the "boilerplate" 35-40% has real, non-obvious correctness subtlety that a naive template would not have gotten right without the SAME review rigor a generator would supposedly save -- three independent hand-written attempts at this "mechanical" pattern produced three different bugs.

THE DECISIVE REASON, though, is upstream of all that: **the remaining two wrappers in this epic's own locked roadmap do not share wrappers 1-3's shape at all.**
- E5.6 (PearPairing, wrapper 4): create/accept/revoke invite -- a handshake/protocol flow with no open-by-name/key, no get/put, no writer/reader concept, no replicate() in the same sense. Nothing like wrappers 1-3's template.
- E5.7 (Autobase, wrapper 5): LOCKED (codex #2) to explicitly NOT expose generic open/apply -- ships prebuilt merge recipes selected by name instead, precisely because wrappers 1-3's flexible single-writer pattern doesn't fit Autobase's multi-writer semantics.

A generator scoped to wrappers 1-3's proven pattern (per the codex audit note's own plan, "the generator, if built, applies from wrapper 4 onward") would have ZERO remaining consumers within this project's actual roadmap -- E5.6 and E5.7 are both confirmed, by their own ticket descriptions, not to fit it. Building one now would be pure speculative investment with no earned repetition ahead of it, which is exactly what this epic's own governing principle forbids ("codegen is EARNED by felt repetition, never a speculative investment").

DECISION: NO-GO, final for this epic's roadmap. No further "re-evaluate after wrapper N" follow-up filed -- there is no wrapper 6 planned, and if a genuinely new corestore-backed, single-writer, replicate-able data structure is ever added to a FUTURE version of this plan, that would be a fresh decision with its own fresh checkpoint, not a resumption of this one.

Full decision also recorded in ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/decisions.jsonl.

5. E5.5 PearDrive (Hyperdrive) on the benchmarked bulk path.
6. E5.4b codegen re-evaluation after the 3rd wrapper: NO-GO, final. Quantified

Every wrapper: typed Dart API (Futures/broadcast Streams/Uint8List/PearKey),

title: E6: Lifecycle + hot-restart correctness
description:
GOAL: Lifecycle and hot-restart correctness — constraints with their own success criteria (locked: "schedule them, don't assume they fall out for free"). This is the first thing every dev hits; it ships in v0.1.

WORK ITEMS:
1. Auto suspend/resume wired to AppLifecycleState with sane linger, overridable (opt-out + manual suspend()/resume() stay public on BareWorklet — packages/flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart already has the state machine; this epic wires the Flutter lifecycle observer + linger timer and pipes real suspend/resume through the native side, which currently no-ops in FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt onMethodCall "suspend"/"resume")
2. Hot restart determinism (locked rule): on attach, compare session nonce + bundle version (contract-spine handshake): reattach when the existing worklet is healthy AND version-matched, otherwise cleanly kill and restart. Never require an app reinstall. The native `started` boolean alone loses authority when the engine detaches (codex #6) — worklet identity must live with the worklet, not the plugin instance.
3. Background reality documentation (codex #8 fold): document loudly what Android actually allows (Doze, OEM task killers, foreground-service option) so an OS-killed swarm isn't blamed on the library. Foreground is the supported guarantee; background is best-effort per OS. Surface suspension state via the X8 connection-state stream so end-user apps can render it.

ACCEPTANCE (from plan success criteria):
- Backgrounding suspends the swarm; foregrounding resumes it; linger window honored; override works
- Hot restart with unchanged bundle -> reattach, session continues, no duplicate worklet
- Hot restart with CHANGED pear-end bundle -> kill + clean restart, no stale-worklet conversation
- Doze/OEM-kill behavior documented per-OS with the foreground-service escape hatch

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Constraints, Next Steps step 5, Success Criteria) and project_plan.md section 6
acceptance_criteria: Background suspends / foreground resumes with linger + override; hot restart: version-matched healthy worklet reattaches, changed bundle kills+restarts cleanly, never reinstall; Doze/OEM-kill reality documented with foreground-service escape hatch; suspension visible on the state stream.
close_reason:
All 5 children closed (E6.1-E6.5), epic acceptance criteria met:

- "Backgrounding suspends the swarm; foregrounding resumes it; linger window honored; override works" -- E6.1 (native suspend/resume wiring, already correct, gained mocked-channel idempotency tests) + E6.2 (PearLifecycle: AppLifecycleState -> linger timer (20s default) -> Pear.suspend/resume, PearLifecyclePolicy.manual override, suspension visible via a new PearRpc.workletSuspendedChanges -> PearSwarmState.suspended path).
- "Hot restart with unchanged bundle -> reattach, session continues, no duplicate worklet" / "changed bundle -> kill + clean restart" -- E6.3 (Pear.start's reattach-or-kill handshake: a new native `reattached` signal distinguishes a genuine reattach from a fresh cold boot, so the short attach-health-probe timeout only applies to the former; an unhealthy OR version-mismatched worklet triggers exactly one kill+restart; a non-timeout failure like a genuine crash propagates immediately instead of being silently retried).
- "Doze/OEM-kill behavior documented per-OS with the foreground-service escape hatch" -- E6.4 (BACKGROUND_EXECUTION.md: Doze/App Standby/OEM battery managers explicitly marked OS-dependent, a foreground-service illustrative sketch, cross-linked from the lifecycle dartdoc).
- "suspension visible on the state stream" -- PearSwarmState.suspended, shipped as part of E6.2, with the resume-target logic (connected/reconnecting/discovering) hardened by that same ticket's own review.

E6.5 (reconnect/delivery contract, a pinned M1 question folded into this epic) additionally resolved and documented in RECONNECT_CONTRACT.md: ephemeral connection objects, no swarm-layer delivery guarantee, with a real write()-silently-resurrects-after-reconnect bug found and fixed along the way (a new PearErrorCode.connectionClosed, checked locally by PearConnection itself).

Every ticket's diff was implemented, unit-tested against flutter_pear_test's conformance-tested fakes (idempotent-transition, full lifecycle, reattach-or-kill, and reconnect-cycle coverage), and independently reviewed via a 3-angle Workflow (correctness/test-rigor/schema-conventions) with adversarial 1-vote verification per finding -- E6.2 found and fixed a dispose-race StateError, a duplicate-notification race, and an errors-must-travel violation; E6.3 found and fixed a critical "attach timeout could never succeed on a slow-but-healthy cold boot" design flaw plus a swallowed-crash bug; E6.5 found and fixed a critical multi-topic regression in its own first-attempt fix, plus a real write()-generation-safety gap in the actual (non-fake) implementation. melos analyze clean and the full Dart suite green (149/149, up from 127 at the start of this epic's work) after every fix, throughout.

DEFERRED (all folded into flutter_pear-doi, the standing hardware-validation tracker): triple hot-restart in a row with an active swarm keeping chat alive on unchanged bundle / correctly kill+restarting on a bumped one; native suspend/resume actually halting/resuming observable swarm activity peer-side; a real wifi->mobile-data switch and airplane-mode blip matching the documented reconnect contract; confirming the native companion-object worklet persistence this session's Dart-level logic sits on top of actually survives a real hot restart (the one thing no Dart-only mocked-channel test can simulate).

title: E3: flutter_pear_test — in-memory fake swarm/core (schema conformance consumer)
description:
GOAL: Build the flutter_pear_test package — an in-memory fake swarm/core that satisfies the RPC schema so (a) all Dart-wrapper work proceeds in parallel with the risky native track, and (b) app devs can unit-test P2P logic without radios or peers. This is a shipped, first-class package (already in the planned monorepo layout), not throwaway test scaffolding.

AUTHORITY RULE (locked): the fake is a CONFORMANCE CONSUMER of the schema. It never defines the contract. If fake and schema disagree, the schema wins and the fake is fixed.

HONESTY RULE (locked, codex #9): the fake is NOT a conformance test of the real system. Matching RPC envelopes says nothing about Hypercore storage semantics, replication, Autobase ordering, swarm races, or crash behavior. Every data-structure package must ALSO ship at least one real-device integration test against the real worklet (those tests live in each wrapper's epic; this epic provides the fake only).

WORK ITEMS:
1. New package packages/flutter_pear_test with its own pubspec, LICENSE (MIT), CHANGELOG, README (pub.dev score rules apply here too — doc every public symbol)
2. In-memory fake implementing the schema surface used by PearSwarm/PearConnection first: join/leave, connection events, data frames, close; deterministic and instant (no real timers where avoidable)
3. Fake failure injection: expose test hooks to simulate worklet crash, call timeout, stale-nonce frame, connection drop, UDP-blocked network (drives the X8 state machine to 'failed') — these exist so the 7 spine failure-path tests and app-dev tests can exercise error handling
4. Grow the fake surface in lockstep as each wrapper epic lands (fake Corestore/Hypercore/Hyperbee/Hyperdrive/pairing/Autobase recipes)
5. Wire into melos: bootstrap, analyze, test all pass from repo root

ACCEPTANCE: an app dev can write a unit test exercising two fake peers exchanging messages, plus every error path, with zero platform channels, zero radios, zero real peers. flutter_pear's own spine tests run against this fake in CI on every push.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Recommended Approach fold-in from C, Next Steps step 2)
acceptance_criteria: App dev can unit-test two fake peers exchanging messages plus every error path with zero radios/peers; failure-injection hooks drive all spine error tests; fake never contradicts schema (schema wins on disagreement).
notes: All 4 children closed. FakeBareWorklet (E3.2) is a genuine conformance consumer of the RPC schema -- PearSwarm/PearConnection run completely unmodified against it, proven by a dedicated cross-package test. All 6 failure-injection hooks (E3.3) exist and each drives a green test proving the app-visible outcome via REAL production PearRpc/PearSwarm/PearConnection, with fake-driven variants covering all of E2.2 through E2.7 living directly in flutter_pear's own spine test files. A CI job (E3.4) now runs the full stack on every push with zero devices, and along the way found + fixed a real melos tooling bug (interactive prompt under packageFilters) that would have made the CI job (and local dev) unreliable. A forward-looking PearSwarm API question (a synchronous connections snapshot, mirroring E2.7's currentState) surfaced while building the fake is tracked separately in flutter_pear-4w6, correctly out of this epic's scope.
close_reason: E3: flutter_pear_test complete. App devs can unit-test two fake peers exchanging messages plus every RPC-spine error path with zero radios/peers/devices -- proven end-to-end via cross-package conformance tests, all 6 failure-injection hooks, and a new CI job running the whole Dart suite on every push. The fake never contradicted the schema; where a design question surfaced (a possible PearSwarm.connections API improvement), it was tracked separately rather than resolved unilaterally inside this epic.

title: PearSwarm._established never prunes closed connections (stale entries accumulate)
description: CONTEXT: Discovered live during flutter_pear-ovt.1.8's real cross-platform (Android emulator <-> iOS simulator) chat smoke test. packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/swarm.dart's _wire() event handler for PearEventName.connectionClose (around line 219-220) only does _byKey.remove(p['peer'])?._close() -- this removes the peer from the _byKey lookup map and marks the PearConnection object closed, but never removes it from the _established list (the backing list for establishedConnections and for Stream.multi's replay-to-first-listener logic in the connections getter, the same list flutter_pear-c2b's fix touches). Consequence: after a peer drops and reconnects, _established keeps BOTH the old closed PearConnection and the new live one forever -- establishedConnections.length grows by 1 on every drop/reconnect cycle and never shrinks. REPRODUCED LIVE: on a real Android emulator + iOS simulator swarm session, after exactly one drop+reconnect, the flutter_pear_example app's _send() (which iterates PearSwarm.establishedConnections and calls conn.write() on each, packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/main.dart line ~370-394) reported 'not delivered to 1 of 2 peers' on TWO separate manual sends after the reconnect, even though only one peer was actually connected -- the write to the stale closed connection throws PearErrorCode.connectionClosed (caught per-connection, so it does not block delivery to the live one) but the confusing '1 of 2' count is user-visible and any code that does connections.length for peer-counting purposes will be wrong. Left unbounded, a long-lived app with many drop/reconnect cycles over a session (mobile networking is inherently flaky) accumulates an ever-growing list of dead PearConnection objects -- a real memory leak, not just cosmetic. FIX: in the connectionClose case, also remove the matching entry from _established (by identity or by the same peer key used for _byKey).
design: Likely a one-line addition alongside _byKey.remove(...) in the connectionClose case: also do _established.removeWhere((c) => c matches the closed peer) or track the PearConnection reference removed from _byKey and remove that exact object from _established by identity (cheaper, avoids a second key-based lookup).
acceptance_criteria: connectionClose removes the corresponding entry from _established (not just _byKey); establishedConnections.length reflects only live connections after a drop+reconnect cycle; a new swarm_test.dart regression test simulates connect -> disconnect -> reconnect via FakeSwarmHub and asserts establishedConnections.length stays at 1, not 2.
notes: CORRECTION after deeper investigation: this is NOT a library bug. packages/flutter_pear/RECONNECT_CONTRACT.md item 4 documents that PearConnection's _closed flag (and write() refusing once closed) was added SPECIFICALLY to prevent a stale PearConnection reference from silently resuming delivery over a peer's new reconnect (the underlying RPC is keyed by peer public key, so without the local _closed check a stale write would reach the peer's NEW connection instead of erroring). establishedConnections' own doc comment explicitly says 'includes connections that have since closed' -- deliberate, since it doubles as the replay buffer for .connections' first-listener semantics (flutter_pear-c2b). Both pieces are intentional, already covered by RECONNECT_CONTRACT.md's test suite. What I actually observed live (flutter_pear_example's _send() reporting 'not delivered to 1 of 2 peers' after a real drop+reconnect on an Android emulator) is the CORRECT, designed-for outcome: one stale PearConnection object's write() correctly threw connectionClosed (caught, non-blocking), the live one's write() succeeded, and the peer DID receive the message (confirmed: iOS logged FLUTTER_PEAR_GATE_RECEIVED for the message sent right after this). The only arguable rough edge is UX phrasing in the DEMO app (packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/main.dart _send(), 'not delivered to N of M peers' counts stale history entries in M, which can read as more alarming than it is) -- a cosmetic demo-app nit, not a correctness issue, not worth a separate fix. Closing as not-a-bug.
close_reason: Investigated and found working as designed -- see correction note. PearConnection's closed-check and establishedConnections' full-history retention are both deliberate (RECONNECT_CONTRACT.md item 4, flutter_pear-c2b's replay contract), already tested. No code change needed.

title: E6 troubleshooting + ERRORS.md: iOS SPM/CocoaPods split, BareKit fetch chain, preflight catalogue, Xcode Cloud note
description:
CONTEXT:
The symptom-first troubleshooting doc is packages/flutter_pear/doc/troubleshooting.md (today Android-only: slow first build, Bare Kit download failure, checksum mismatch, native-binary load, manifest merger, emulator connectivity, backgrounding). The error catalog is packages/flutter_pear/ERRORS.md (four-part entries: problem/cause/fix/doc anchor per error code). Upstream facts to cite inline (already verified during planning, do not re-research): CocoaPods issue 2187 — prepare_command never runs for path-installed pods, and Flutter installs plugin pods exactly that way, which is why the compat podspec fetches BareKit in a script_phase; flutter/flutter issue 187710 — SPM URL binaryTargets have a known Xcode Cloud cache-collision failure; flutter issue 186054 — SPM binary-artifact cache-resolution footgun; holepunchto/bare-kit v2.3.0 upstream releases ship ONE multi-platform prebuilds.zip (~354MB), so the maintainer repacks BareKit.xcframework and rehosts it as a release asset on THIS repo, and barekit-pin.json (TO BE CREATED by the packaging epic; locate with find) carries a two-link checksum chain: upstream zip sha256 then repacked asset sha256. The branded build preflights (arch/slice mismatch; deployment target below BareKit minimum) were created by the packaging/host epic — grep packages/flutter_pear_bare/ios (TO BE CREATED by that epic) for the exact message strings, and read that epic's bd notes for the D18 ordering outcome (whether the preflight fires before SwiftPM resolution errors).

DO:
1. Add a top-level iOS section to doc/troubleshooting.md split into two subsections, on SPM and on CocoaPods, so an error lookup never debugs the wrong packaging system. Open the section with the one command that tells a consumer which packaging path they are on, and the minimum Flutter version for the SPM path (from the packaging epic notes / COMPATIBILITY.md).
2. BareKit fetch failures entry: what to check when the repacked release asset or the upstream URL is unreachable (walk both links of the pin chain in barekit-pin.json); checksum-mismatch guidance; document the FLUTTER_PEAR_BAREKIT_URL override (mirror URL or local file path, checksum still enforced) and a manual-vendoring recipe for enterprise-proxy/air-gapped builds — confirm the override env var name against the packaging epic implementation before documenting.
3. Preflight error catalogue: one entry per branded preflight failure — arch/slice mismatch (Intel-Mac simulators unsupported; arm64-only sim slices is a standing decision, the x86_64 slice ships later only on demand) and consumer deployment target below BareKit minimum — each quoting the VERBATIM Xcode error text plus the one-line fix. Record the D18 ordering outcome; if preflight cannot fire before SwiftPM resolution errors on the SPM path, add entries keyed to the raw SPM errors.
4. Xcode Cloud entry: cache-collision failure (flutter/flutter issue 187710), the CocoaPods-fallback workaround, and that SPM resolution requires network access.
5. SPM verbatim-error entries: checksum mismatch and the 186054 cache footgun, quoting exact messages and the script_phase failure text including its fix line.
6. ERRORS.md additions: an entry keying an iOS worklet boot failure to a custom bundle built without the iOS host flags (the advanced custom-worklet path; give the bare-pack host flags used by dart run flutter_pear:pack — read them from packages/flutter_pear/bin/pack.dart); an entry for the symlink/deep-path mirror-rejection warning emitted when mirroring untrusted drives (grep packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/schema.dart and packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js for the code name landed by the earlier epic) so a dev debugging file-never-arrived sees why.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- troubleshooting.md has the iOS section with distinct on SPM and on CocoaPods subsections and the path-detection command.
- BareKit fetch entry covers both chain links plus the env override and vendoring recipe.
- Preflight, Xcode Cloud, and SPM entries quote verbatim error text with fixes; D18 ordering outcome reflected.
- ERRORS.md gains the custom-bundle-iOS-host entry and the mirror-rejection entry in four-part format.

AUTO-VALIDATION:
- grep -c "on SPM" /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear/doc/troubleshooting.md returns at least 1 and grep -c "on CocoaPods" returns at least 1; grep -c 187710 returns at least 1; grep -c FLUTTER_PEAR_BAREKIT_URL returns at least 1 (adjust the var name to the implemented one).
- grep -ci "custom bundle" /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear/ERRORS.md returns at least 1; grep -ci symlink returns at least 1.

REVIEW BEFORE CLOSE:
1. Run every AUTO-VALIDATION command; paste a short evidence summary into this issue with bd update <this-issue-id> --append-notes (never bare --notes, it overwrites).
2. Run melos run analyze from the repo root and the test suite of every touched package (flutter test from the package dir; for pear-end changes run npm test in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end).
3. Review the implementation: read the full git diff and check it line-by-line against every ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA bullet; fix any gap before closing. If a criterion cannot be met, record why with --append-notes and flag with bd human instead of closing.
4. NEVER run git commit or git push — leave all changes uncommitted in the working tree for the developer.
5. Close with bd close <this-issue-id> --reason and a one-line summary. If you discovered follow-up work beyond this task's scope, file a NEW bd issue for it instead of expanding this one.

CONSTRAINTS: do not relitigate the standing v0.2 decisions (sim-tier validation, hardened demo gates release, committed addons, single flutter_pear_bare package, no CI, arm64-only sim slices, platformInfo ships both fields).

title: Measure and record example-app APK/IPA size deltas versus v0.1 (eng 6A) into the epic notes
description:
CONTEXT: eng-review 6A requires a consumer-impact disclosure: the measured size cost of flutter_pear v0.2 on the example app, recorded in the epic's bd notes and consumed by the docs epic for the README/FAQ/changelog disclosure (related: decision D20 accept-and-disclose -- pub downloads all dependencies regardless of target platform per flutter/flutter#130210, which is why the committed iOS artifacts grow even Android-only consumers' downloads; the docs epic writes that text, THIS task only produces the numbers). The v0.1 baseline exists as git tag v0.0.1 (verified). The example app is packages/flutter_pear_example (its ios/ runner is created by the example-app epic; if flutter build ipa is impossible because ios/ is still absent, record blocked with bd --append-notes and bd human). The epic issue to receive the numbers is flutter_pear-ovt.5. APK inspection precedent: unzip -l build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-debug.apk sorted by size.
DO:
1. Create packages/flutter_pear_example/tool/measure_size_delta.sh (TO BE CREATED) that: (a) builds the CURRENT example: flutter build apk --release and flutter build ipa --no-codesign from packages/flutter_pear_example, recording the APK byte size and the .xcarchive's Runner.app total size (du -sk) plus the generated IPA size if produced; (b) builds the v0.1 BASELINE without disturbing the working tree: git worktree add to a temp dir at tag v0.0.1, melos bootstrap there, hydrate the example runner if needed (flutter create --platforms=android . -- the v0.1 example was Android-only), flutter build apk --release, record the APK size, then git worktree remove the temp dir; (c) prints a fixed-format table: apk_v0.1_bytes, apk_v0.2_bytes, apk_delta_bytes, ios_app_v0.2_bytes (absolute -- no v0.1 iOS baseline exists, Android-only release; say so explicitly in the output), build modes, Flutter version, and date.
2. Run it and append the table to the epic issue: bd update flutter_pear-ovt.5 --append-notes with the table text (never bare --notes; also avoid backticks in the note text -- double-quoted shell strings run backtick substitution).
3. Note in the script header that the docs epic consumes these numbers for the 6A disclosure and the D20 changelog size line.
ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- measure_size_delta.sh exists, is re-runnable, cleans up its git worktree even on failure (trap), and never modifies the main working tree.
- The printed table contains the v0.1 APK size, v0.2 APK size, APK delta, and the absolute v0.2 iOS app size with the no-baseline caveat.
- The numbers are recorded on flutter_pear-ovt.5 via --append-notes in the fixed format.
- Release vs debug build mode is stated alongside every number.
AUTO-VALIDATION:
- bash /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear_example/tool/measure_size_delta.sh -- expect exit 0 and the fixed-format table on stdout.
- git -C /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear worktree list -- expect only the main worktree remains after the run.
- bd show flutter_pear-ovt.5 -- expect the appended size table visible in the notes.

title: flutter_pear_test fake parity: Pear.platformInfo (both fields) + drive-mirror warning event, parity-tested
description:
CONTEXT: app devs unit-test against FakeBareWorklet (packages/flutter_pear_test/lib/src/fake_worklet.dart, ~73KB -- it currently has NO platformInfo support, verified by grep) so every new real-path surface needs fake parity. Two v0.2 surfaces: (1) Pear.platformInfo (plan 6.2 / DX-T4, implemented in the API epic -- grep packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/pear.dart and lib/src/schema.dart for platformInfo; if not landed, record blocked with bd --append-notes and bd human). Standing decision D17 + DX2 decision 55: BOTH fields ship -- backgroundExecution is an enum (foregroundOnly or bestEffort; a bool cannot express linger reality) and validationTier (simulator, emulator, or device) has pinned semantics: the validation basis of the RELEASE, updated only at release. The plan requires the fake to expose it too so app tests can exercise both platforms in code. (2) The drive-mirror warning event (typed warning emitted when symlink/deep-path entries are rejected during mirror-to-disk; wire shape defined by the pack epic in pear-end and lib/src/schema.dart). Parity precedent: packages/flutter_pear/test/fake_conformance_test.dart deliberately lives in flutter_pear (the fake is a conformance CONSUMER of the schema; that package imports the fake only for this proof). Failure-injection precedent: packages/flutter_pear_test/lib/src/failure_injection.dart.
DO:
1. Read the real platformInfo implementation (RPC method name in schema.dart, response field names, Dart value type) and the mirror-warning event shape.
2. In FakeBareWorklet, implement the platformInfo RPC method returning configurable values: default sensible values plus public setters so a test can simulate an iOS run (backgroundExecution foregroundOnly) and an Android run -- both platforms exercisable without a device. Match field names and encodings byte-for-byte with the real schema.
3. Implement the drive-mirror warning event on the fake's mirror-to-disk path: when the fake mirror encounters a symlink or traversal entry (or when injected via the failure_injection pattern), emit the identical event shape the real pear-end emits.
4. Extend packages/flutter_pear/test/fake_conformance_test.dart (or add fake_platform_info_conformance_test.dart alongside it in flutter_pear/test/): Pear.platformInfo against the fake returns the typed value with BOTH fields; the fake's warning event decodes into the same typed Dart surface the real-path test uses; streams are broadcast.
5. Add dartdoc for every new public symbol in flutter_pear_test (pub.dev score target).
ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- FakeBareWorklet answers the platformInfo method with the same shape as pear-end; both fields present; values configurable from tests.
- Fake emits the drive-mirror warning event with field names identical to the real path.
- Parity/conformance test in flutter_pear passes against the fake for both surfaces.
- Every new public symbol documented.
AUTO-VALIDATION:
- cd /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear_test && flutter test -- expect all pass.
- cd /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear && flutter test test/fake_conformance_test.dart (plus the new parity file if separate) -- expect all pass.
- Mutation spot-check: change one response field-name string in the fake's platformInfo handler, rerun the parity test -- expect FAIL; restore the fake.
REVIEW BEFORE CLOSE:
1. Run every AUTO-VALIDATION command; paste a short evidence summary into this issue with bd update <this-issue-id> --append-notes (never bare --notes, it overwrites).
2. Run melos run analyze from the repo root and the test suite of every touched package (flutter test from the package dir; for pear-end changes run npm test in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end).
3. Review the implementation: read the full git diff and check it line-by-line against every ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA bullet; fix any gap before closing. If a criterion cannot be met, record why with --append-notes and flag with bd human instead of closing.
4. NEVER run git commit or git push -- leave all changes uncommitted in the working tree for the developer.
5. Close with bd close <this-issue-id> --reason and a one-line summary. If you discovered follow-up work beyond this task's scope, file a NEW bd issue for it instead of expanding this one.
CONSTRAINTS: Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, no callbacks; every public symbol documented; do not relitigate the standing v0.2 decisions (platformInfo ships both fields); all P2P logic stays in JavaScript inside the Bare worklet and Dart is a typed remote control.
notes: BLOCKED (flagged bd human), per this task's own prerequisite check: grepped lib/src/pear.dart and lib/src/schema.dart for 'platformInfo' -- zero matches, the real API-epic surface (plan 6.2/DX-T4, D17/DX2#55) has not landed at all. The drive-mirror warning event (this task's second surface) was ALSO just confirmed absent while working flutter_pear-ovt.5.7 in this same session (no mirror-warning event in schema.dart, no symlink handling in pear-end/index.js's DRIVE_MIRROR_TO_DISK). Both of this task's two target surfaces are unimplemented in the real (non-fake) path, so there is nothing for FakeBareWorklet to achieve PARITY WITH yet -- implementing fake-side platformInfo/warning-event support ahead of the real API would invert the parity relationship this task exists to enforce (the fake mirrors the real schema, not the other way around) and risks the fake diverging from whatever the real API epic eventually lands. Nothing implemented in flutter_pear_test; no test written. Developer decision needed: prioritize the API epic (Pear.platformInfo) and the E2 pack epic (drive-mirror warning event) first -- both are real prerequisites this task depends on -- or explicitly defer 5.9 until they land.

title: 5A: hostile-drive mirroring tests -- JS symlink/deep-path rejection + Dart warning-event surfacing
description:
CONTEXT: Eng2 #6 found a zip-slip vector: mirror-drive propagates remote symlink entries and Localdrive writes relative link targets uncontained, so a malicious peer's drive can write outside the local staging dir during Method.DRIVE_MIRROR_TO_DISK (handler at packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js:980-992, today a plain drive.mirror(localDrive) with add/change/remove counts). The FIX (reject/skip symlink entries and path-traversal names when mirroring untrusted drives, emitting a typed warning event so a dev debugging a file-never-arrived mystery can see why -- DX2 decision 54) is implemented in the PACK epic, NOT here. THIS task writes the tests. PREREQUISITE CHECK: grep packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js for symlink handling in the mirror path and grep packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/schema.dart for the mirror-warning event name; if the rejection or the Dart surface has not landed, record blocked with bd --append-notes and bd human -- do NOT implement the pear-end change in this task. Test harness precedents: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/test/index.test.js drives the worklet handler in-process; npm test in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end runs node --test --test-concurrency=1. Dart RPC decode-path test precedent: packages/flutter_pear/test/rpc_test.dart; drive wrapper tests: packages/flutter_pear/test/drive_test.dart.
DO:
1. Create packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/test/hostile-drive-mirror.test.js (TO BE CREATED, node --test style): construct a writer Hyperdrive in a temp Corestore containing (a) a symlink entry whose target escapes the mirror root (for example ../../escape.txt) and (b) an entry with a path-traversal name, plus one benign file; replicate it to a reader and invoke the DRIVE_MIRROR_TO_DISK code path the way index.test.js drives handlers; assert nothing is written outside the temp staging root (scan the parent dir), the hostile entries are skipped, the benign file mirrors, and the typed warning event frame is emitted with its documented shape (event name, entry path, reason) for EACH rejected entry.
2. Create or extend a Dart test (packages/flutter_pear/test/drive_test.dart or a new drive_mirror_warning_test.dart): feed a synthetic warning event frame through the RPC decode path (rpc_test.dart pattern) and assert the public warning surface on the Dart side (the new PearEventName member and its broadcast Stream -- use the exact names found in lib/src/schema.dart) emits a typed value carrying path and reason.
3. Keep both tests hermetic: temp dirs only, cleaned up in teardown (see rejoin-replay.test.js for the tmpdir pattern).
ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- JS test proves: no write escapes the staging root; hostile entries skipped; benign file mirrored; one typed warning per rejected entry with the documented shape.
- Dart test proves the warning event decodes into the typed public surface and the stream is broadcast.
- Both tests are hermetic (no leftover temp state).
AUTO-VALIDATION:
- cd /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear/pear-end && node --test test/hostile-drive-mirror.test.js -- expect pass; then npm test -- expect the full suite green.
- cd /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear && flutter test test/drive_test.dart (plus the new file if separate) -- expect pass.
- Mutation spot-check JS: invert the symlink-rejection condition in pear-end/index.js so symlinks mirror again, rerun the JS test -- expect FAIL; restore with git checkout -- pear-end/index.js.
- Mutation spot-check Dart: change the expected event-name string in the Dart test to a wrong value, rerun -- expect FAIL; revert the test edit.
REVIEW BEFORE CLOSE:
1. Run every AUTO-VALIDATION command; paste a short evidence summary into this issue with bd update <this-issue-id> --append-notes (never bare --notes, it overwrites).
2. Run melos run analyze from the repo root and the test suite of every touched package (flutter test from the package dir; for pear-end changes run npm test in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end).
3. Review the implementation: read the full git diff and check it line-by-line against every ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA bullet; fix any gap before closing. If a criterion cannot be met, record why with --append-notes and flag with bd human instead of closing.
4. NEVER run git commit or git push -- leave all changes uncommitted in the working tree for the developer.
5. Close with bd close <this-issue-id> --reason and a one-line summary. If you discovered follow-up work beyond this task's scope, file a NEW bd issue for it instead of expanding this one.
CONSTRAINTS: all P2P logic stays in JavaScript inside the Bare worklet and Dart is a typed remote control (never reimplement protocols in Dart); Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, no callbacks; new dependencies must be MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC (prefer zero new deps -- node:test is built in).
notes: BLOCKED (flagged bd human), per this task's own explicit prerequisite check: grepped packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js's DRIVE_MIRROR_TO_DISK handler (line 980) for symlink handling -- none exists, it is still 'a plain drive.mirror(localDrive) with add/change/remove counts' exactly as the CONTEXT section describes the pre-fix state. Grepped packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/schema.dart for any mirror-warning event name -- none exists. Neither the pear-end zip-slip fix (Eng2 #6) nor the Dart-side typed warning surface (DX2 decision 54) has landed; both are explicitly scoped to the E2 pack epic (flutter_pear-ovt.2), not this task. Per this task's own instruction ('do NOT implement the pear-end change in this task'), nothing was implemented and no test was written against a nonexistent handler/event -- a hostile-drive test suite asserting rejection behavior that doesn't exist yet would either fail immediately (accurately, but uselessly) or require me to write the security fix myself, which is out of this task's bounds and belongs to flutter_pear-ovt.2. Developer decision needed: prioritize flutter_pear-ovt.2's pear-end symlink-filter work first (this task's real prerequisite), or explicitly defer 5.7 until it lands. Nothing touched in the repo.

title: 5A: hot-restart generation/stale-exit coverage exercised for iOS (flutter_pear-3vh machinery)
description:
CONTEXT: the hot-restart reattach-or-kill machinery (bd issue flutter_pear-3vh) lives in packages/flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart: a per-instance _generationId captured from the start result (line 151), a STATIC MethodChannel flutter_pear_bare/control shared across generations (lines 75-76, 203-206), and a stale-generation drop in the native-call handler (lines 210-215: a buffered message stamped with an older generationId is silently ignored). The Kotlin host (packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/src/main/kotlin/tech/loable/flutter_pear_bare/FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt) is the behavioral spec the new Swift host replicates. Eng2 #8 finding: this machinery has NO iOS automated coverage; hot-restart-during-message-burst is a MANUAL T2 checklist item. Prerequisite: the Swift host must exist under packages/flutter_pear_bare/ios/ (TO BE CREATED by the Swift-host epic; the example app's ios/ runner likewise -- neither exists today). If absent when you start, record blocked with bd --append-notes and bd human. Existing Dart tests: packages/flutter_pear_bare/test/bare_worklet_test.dart.
DO:
1. Extend packages/flutter_pear_bare/test/bare_worklet_test.dart with channel-level tests simulating the SWIFT host's message sequences (read the Swift host and diff its control-channel behavior against the Kotlin host first): a workletExited (or equivalent crash-report) call stamped with the CURRENT generationId surfaces on the onCrash stream; the same call stamped with a STALE generationId is dropped silently by the new BareWorklet instance after a simulated hot restart (create instance A, capture its generation, start instance B, inject A's exit message); the reattach start path adopts the existing generationId from the start result. Use TestDefaultBinaryMessengerBinding to mock outgoing calls and inject incoming native-to-Dart calls.
2. Create packages/flutter_pear_example/tool/ios_hot_restart_check.sh (TO BE CREATED): boot an available iOS simulator via xcrun simctl (pick the first available iPhone from xcrun simctl list devices available), run flutter run -d <sim-udid> --pid-file /tmp/fp_hot_restart.pid from packages/flutter_pear_example capturing stdout to a log, wait (with timeout) for the app's worklet-attached marker in the log (find the exact marker string the example app prints on successful attach -- see packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/main.dart status wiring, and the marker pattern precedent in packages/flutter_pear/tool/fresh_machine_check.sh), then send kill -USR2 to the flutter tool pid (SIGUSR2 = hot restart), and assert the attach marker appears AGAIN after restart with no crash marker; exit nonzero on any timeout.
3. Document in the script header that the message-burst variant remains MANUAL per Eng2 #8.
ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Stale-generation drop, current-generation crash surfacing, and reattach-adopts-generation each have a dedicated Dart test.
- ios_hot_restart_check.sh runs green on this Mac against the simulator: attach marker seen, SIGUSR2 restart, marker seen again.
- MANUAL: hot-restart-during-message-burst noted as a manual T2 checklist item in the script header (not automated).
AUTO-VALIDATION:
- cd /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear_bare && flutter test test/bare_worklet_test.dart -- expect all pass.
- Mutation spot-check: comment out the stale-generation early return in lib/src/bare_worklet.dart (the return at the line reading if (_generationId != null && callGeneration != _generationId) return;), rerun -- the stale-drop test must FAIL; restore with git checkout -- lib/src/bare_worklet.dart.
- bash /Volumes/mandark-1Tb/projects/loabletech/flutter_pear/packages/flutter_pear_example/tool/ios_hot_restart_check.sh -- expect exit 0 with both attach markers logged.
REVIEW BEFORE CLOSE:
1. Run every AUTO-VALIDATION command; paste a short evidence summary into this issue with bd update <this-issue-id> --append-notes (never bare --notes, it overwrites).
2. Run melos run analyze from the repo root and the test suite of every touched package (flutter test from the package dir; for pear-end changes run npm test in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end).
3. Review the implementation: read the full git diff and check it line-by-line against every ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA bullet; fix any gap before closing. If a criterion cannot be met, record why with --append-notes and flag with bd human instead of closing.
4. NEVER run git commit or git push -- leave all changes uncommitted in the working tree for the developer.
5. Close with bd close <this-issue-id> --reason and a one-line summary. If you discovered follow-up work beyond this task's scope, file a NEW bd issue for it instead of expanding this one.
CONSTRAINTS: Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, no callbacks; all P2P logic stays in JavaScript inside the Bare worklet and Dart is a typed remote control (never reimplement protocols in Dart); do not relitigate the standing v0.2 decisions (sim-tier validation).
notes:
PARTIAL PROGRESS + flagged bd human for the remainder. DO step 1 (Dart channel-level tests) DONE: bare_worklet_test.dart already had solid stale-generation-drop + current-generation-crash-surfacing coverage (a pre-existing test exercising both halves); the one genuinely missing piece was 'reattach adopts the native-echoed generationId, not a fresh/invented one' -- added that test. First attempt modeled it wrong (two sequential BareWorklet.start() calls with the first still running) and caught a real, useful thing about this harness: BareWorklet.start() short-circuits to the SAME existing instance without even calling native 'start' again when one is already running (bare_worklet.dart:139-143), so a second mocked response is never actually exercised that way. Fixed by modeling it the same way pear_test.dart's own reattach tests do: a single start() call where the mock reports reattached:true+generationId:7 directly (simulating, from this fresh test/isolate's view, that this IS the first native call it makes but the native side says it was already running -- exactly what a real post-hot-restart Dart isolate sees). AUTO-VALIDATION: flutter test test/bare_worklet_test.dart -> 16/16 pass. Mutation spot-check (commented out the stale-generation early-return at bare_worklet.dart:215): the stale-drop test correctly FAILED; restored via git checkout -- (confirmed safe, file was unmodified before). melos run analyze clean (3/3). DO step 2 (packages/flutter_pear_example/tool/ios_hot_restart_check.sh) BLOCKED, flagged bd human: per this task's own prerequisite, needs a real Swift host under packages/flutter_pear_bare/ios/ (does not exist -- confirmed via test -d) and a real productized example-app iOS integration (not the flutter_pear-ovt.1.4/1.7 throwaway SpikeBareHost.swift spike living directly in the example app's own Runner, which is explicitly NOT the same thing per the epic's own repeated 'never packages/flutter_pear_bare/ios' constraint). Additionally, even the SPIKE host wouldn't support this script meaningfully: SpikeBareHost.swift's 'start' handler unconditionally returns reattached:false/generationId:1 -- it has no real reattach-or-kill implementation at all yet (that's flutter_pear-ovt.1.10's separate, not-yet-done scope, itself blocked behind the physical-Android task flutter_pear-ovt.1.8 which is itself bd-human-flagged). Writing a shell script that boots a simulator and asserts an 'attach marker reappears after SIGUSR2' against a host that always reports fresh boots would be testing nothing real. Not closing: acceptance criteria require both pieces.
