Cross-linked from the lifecycle API dartdoc per this ticket's own STEP 3: PearLifecycle's class doc, Pear.suspend's doc, and PearSwarmState.suspended's doc each now reference BACKGROUND_EXECUTION.md (plain backtick filename reference, matching drive.dart's existing precedent for referencing BENCHMARK.md -- dartdoc [Foo] square brackets only resolve within the importing library, so a cross-file, non-symbol reference like this uses backticks, not brackets).

VALIDATION (doc reviewed against E6.1-E6.3's actual, tested behavior, no aspirational claims): every claim about PearLifecycle/Pear.suspend/Pear.resume/hot-restart was checked line-by-line against the actual implementation and the tests that cover it (the 20s linger default, the quick-switch-never-thrashes guarantee, the three-way connected/reconnecting/discovering resume-target logic including the E6.2 review's own _everConnected fix, the E6.3 reattach-or-kill mechanics). Every Doze/App-Standby/OEM-killer/foreground-service claim is explicitly marked OS-dependent or "not a flutter_pear API" rather than presented as tested library behavior.

melos analyze: clean, 4/4 packages (no new code, only markdown + 3 dartdoc cross-reference additions). melos run test --no-select: 147/147 green (unchanged, no logic touched). No Workflow-based multi-agent review run for this ticket -- the diff is prose plus 3 one-line doc cross-references, not new logic, and every factual claim was independently fact-checked by hand against the just-closed, deeply-verified E6.1-E6.3 implementation rather than needing a correctness-bug-hunting review.

DISCOVERED WORTH A NEW BEAD (not a new bead -- folded into the existing one): packages/flutter_pear/README.md still says "Early / pre-release. M0 (worklet echo) is scaffolded..." -- badly stale relative to actual progress (E1-E6.4 substantially implemented and closed). Flagged on flutter_pear-ph3.8 (the CLAUDE.md/project_plan.md reconciliation ticket already tracking this exact class of drift) so the README gets the same fix when that ticket lands.

No hardware leg to defer for this ticket specifically (it's a doc, not new testable behavior) -- the doc's own "What's deferred" section already points at flutter_pear-doi for the underlying E6.1-E6.3 claims it describes.

title: E5.4: Codegen decision checkpoint (after 2 wrappers)
description:
CONTEXT: E5 checkpoint (locked decision gate). After two hand-written wrappers, assess: is the wrapper boilerplate provably mechanical and repetitive? Codegen is EARNED by felt repetition, never a speculative investment.
STEPS:
1. Diff-review store.dart vs bee.dart + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts; quantify the mechanical fraction (lines that are pattern-stamped vs semantic).
2. Write the decision as a bd comment on epic E5 AND a gstack decision log entry: GO (build a minimal generator for wrapper 4+: what it generates, what stays handwritten) or NO-GO (keep hand-writing; name what changed vs expectations).
3. If GO: file a new bead for the generator with its scope pinned to exactly the mechanical fraction identified — the generator NEVER generates semantic API decisions.
VALIDATION: decision comment posted with the quantified evidence; if GO, generator bead exists with fenced scope; no generator code written inside THIS task.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Quantified mechanical-vs-semantic assessment posted on the epic; explicit GO/NO-GO recorded in decision log; GO spawns a scope-fenced generator bead; no generator built in this task.
notes: CODEX AUDIT NOTE (judgment call, keeping the checkpoint after wrapper 2 with this clarification): the locked decision reads 'only introduce codegen if, BY THE THIRD wrapper, boilerplate is provably mechanical.' Therefore: this checkpoint may conclude GO-pending — in which case PearDrive (E5.5) is STILL written by hand and the generator, if built, applies from wrapper 4 (pairing) onward only. A GO here never causes wrapper 3 to be generated.
close_reason: E5.4 decision: NO-GO for codegen right now. Diff-reviewed store.dart vs bee.dart + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts (line counts and structural comparison posted in full on epic E5's notes). Mechanical fraction estimated ~35-40% (open validation, reopen-after-close guard, error wrapping, replicate/close idempotency, fake writer+merge+handshake, schema shape, several near-identical test cases) -- real but not dominant, and critically: 100% of the 3 real bugs E5.3's review caught lived in the semantic/bespoke fraction (the range/watch subscribe-lifecycle, the actual data-model translation) a generator would never touch. Only 2 samples exist and the original locked decision's own threshold is 'by the third wrapper' -- this checkpoint intentionally ran one wrapper early per its own design. No generator built (per VALIDATION). Decision recorded both as a bd comment on flutter_pear-2vz and in ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/decisions.jsonl. Filed flutter_pear-y3g (blocked on E5.5) to redo this with 3 real samples once Hyperdrive's genuinely different file-tree API lands. No code changed by this task -- melos analyze still green across all 4 packages.

title: repackBareKit produces a different zip sha256 on every run for byte-identical BareKit.xcframework content
description: Observed during flutter_pear-ovt.3.1: running dart run flutter_pear:pack --repack-barekit --no-upload twice in a row (no upstream change) produced two different repackedSha256 values in barekit-pin.json (ec34fbd6... then a83a8d68...). The rezip step (Process.run zip -qr ... BareKit.xcframework) is not byte-deterministic, likely due to filesystem metadata (mtimes) captured from a fresh unzip each run. This undermines the two-link checksum chain's assumption that the repacked asset is a stable, reproducible artifact -- a future :pack --repack-barekit re-run (e.g. to rotate an expired upload) would produce a NEW checksum even though the BareKit bytes did not change, forcing every consumer (Package.swift, podspec) to update in lockstep for no real reason. Fix: normalize file mtimes (e.g. touch -t a fixed epoch, or zip -X to strip extra fields) before zipping in repackBareKit, then add a regression test asserting two repack runs against the same downloadFn-injected bytes produce identical output zip checksums.
notes:
ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED empirically: a controlled test fixture (identical file content, zipped 1.5s apart with plain zip -qr) reproduced two different checksums; adding touch -t <fixed epoch> before zipping alone was enough to make them match in that test, but real Apple framework binaries also tend to carry extended attributes (code-signing/quarantine metadata) zip stores as 'extra fields' -- added zip -X (strip extra fields) as well for defense-in-depth, verified the combination together.

FIX in packages/flutter_pear/bin/pack.dart's repackBareKit: added _normalizeMtimesForZip(repackedXcfw) (new helper, shells out to touch -t 202001010000 across every file+directory under the repack tree -- dart:io's File.setLastModifiedSync has no Directory equivalent, confirmed by a real analyzer error when first tried) right before the zip step, and changed the zip invocation from -qr to -qrX.

TESTS: added a new regression test to packages/flutter_pear/test/pack_ios_test.dart's existing 'BareKit repack' group -- runs repackBareKit twice (via the same downloadFn/uploadFn injection pattern the existing tests already use, no real network) against a richer, multi-file/multi-directory fixture (closer to BareKit's real xcframework shape than the existing single-file fixture, to also guard against a zip-entry-ORDER-dependent non-determinism, not just mtimes), with a real 2-second gap between runs, and asserts both repackedSha256 values in the written pin file match.

Mutation spot-check: commented out the _normalizeMtimesForZip call -- the new test correctly failed (two genuinely different checksums, confirmed by the assertion diff). Restored, reran -- passes again.

REAL end-to-end verification (matching this bug's own original repro exactly, not just the injected-fixture test): ran dart run flutter_pear:pack --repack-barekit --no-upload twice in a row against the real upstream holepunchto/bare-kit v2.3.0 release. Both runs produced the IDENTICAL repackedSha256 (bb54259f54078cca69f54d868f36b3c2f72c95fcf5bca29db862f695f05a4ba7) -- confirmed via diff on the two runs' barekit-pin.json. Reverted both runs' barekit-pin.json/Package.swift changes afterward (verification only, not an intended pin bump) -- git status confirms clean.

melos run analyze: 0 issues, all 4 packages. melos run test --no-select: 255 passed, 0 failed (254 + 1 new).
close_reason: Fixed: repackBareKit now normalizes every file/directory mtime to a fixed epoch (touch -t, since dart:io has no Directory.setLastModifiedSync) and zips with -X (strip extra fields/extended attributes) before computing the checksum, making the archive a pure function of file content. Verified via a new regression test (two repacks of identical injected fixture content, 2s apart, same checksum) plus a real end-to-end run of the actual CLI tool against real upstream BareKit twice in a row -- identical checksums both times, confirming the original bug's exact repro no longer reproduces.

title: release_gate.sh's fresh-machine gate always targets the permanently-attached BYD device
description:
CONTEXT: this dev machine has a physical BYD DiLink automotive head unit (an unrelated project's hardware, BladeWatch) permanently reachable at adb 192.168.0.251:5555. tool/release_gate.sh's fresh-machine gate (flutter_pear-ovt.5.12) targets whatever device is already attached before considering booting an AVD emulator, so as long as this device stays connected, the gate will deterministically fail with the already-understood libnativehelper.so ROM issue (flutter_pear-ovt.1.8) instead of exercising a device that can actually complete the TTHW probe.
DO: consider a way for release_gate.sh (or fresh_machine_check.sh itself) to prefer/require a known-working emulator over an already-attached device that's previously failed, e.g. an env var allowlist/denylist of serials, or a --prefer-emulator flag. Non-blocking -- today's workaround is simply disconnecting the BYD device before running the gate, or accepting the FAIL with its documented cause.
ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: the fresh-machine gate can be pointed at a healthy device/emulator even when an unrelated, known-bad device is also attached.
notes:
Implemented the env-var-override option from this task's own suggested fixes (simplest of the offered options -- an explicit allowlist-of-one, not a full allow/denylist or --prefer-emulator auto-heuristic, matching this task's own 'Non-blocking' priority).

RELEASE_GATE_ADB_SERIAL=<serial> tool/release_gate.sh: the fresh-machine gate's target-selection now checks this env var first -- if set and that serial is actually in the 'device' state per adb devices, it's used directly, bypassing the previous first-match-wins selection entirely (the BYD reliably wins that race since it's connected long before any emulator this gate boots). If set but that serial ISN'T currently in 'device' state, fails loudly naming exactly what was expected, rather than silently falling back to the old behavior. Unset (the default): unchanged first-match behavior, so this is purely additive.

Documented in the script's own top-of-file Usage block, matching its existing --list/--only documentation style.

VERIFICATION: no Android device or emulator was attached to this machine during this session (adb devices returned empty), so a full, real end-to-end gate_fresh-machine run against a live BYD+emulator combination wasn't possible. Verified the actual selection logic in isolation instead, piping realistic fake List of devices attached output (a BYD-style serial + an emulator-style serial, both in 'device' state) through the exact same awk/grep pipeline added to the script: (1) RELEASE_GATE_ADB_SERIAL set to the emulator serial correctly selects it over the BYD entry; (2) RELEASE_GATE_ADB_SERIAL set to a serial not present correctly detects no match (the FAIL path); (3) unset reproduces the original bug's exact behavior (first-listed serial -- the BYD -- wins), confirming the fix is a real behavior change, not a no-op. bash -n on the full script: no syntax errors.
close_reason: Added RELEASE_GATE_ADB_SERIAL env var override for the fresh-machine gate's device targeting -- when set, it's used directly instead of adb devices' first-match-wins selection (which reliably picks the permanently-attached BYD device over a freshly-booted emulator). Verified the selection logic against simulated multi-device adb output; no real hardware/emulator was available this session for a full live run.

title: SECURITY_POSTURE.md: uninstall wording slightly imprecise re: Android Backup Manager edge case
description: Real-hardware finding (flutter_pear-doi backup-exclusion validation): with Android Backup Manager enabled and an active backup dataset for the app (not the default state, but reachable), uninstall+reinstall auto-restored non-identity files (e.g. a plain marker file) via Android install-time auto-restore, even though pear-corestore/pear-bulk correctly never came back through that same path. SECURITY_POSTURE.md said flatly "uninstalling the app deletes everything", which is very slightly imprecise for that edge case.
acceptance_criteria: SECURITY_POSTURE.md's uninstall section clarifies the guarantee is specifically about identity/replication data (pear-corestore/pear-bulk), not literally every byte, without weakening the core claim.
notes: FIXED (2026-07-05): reworded packages/flutter_pear/SECURITY_POSTURE.md (the "What survives reinstall" section) to note the real-hardware-verified nuance directly. No code change needed.
close_reason: Fixed same-day as filed -- reworded SECURITY_POSTURE.md per the real-hardware finding, no code change needed.

title: E9.7 follow-up: README/CHANGELOG per package are stale relative to actual E1-E6 status
description:
E9.7's STEP 3 (verify LICENSE/CHANGELOG/README per package) confirmed all three publishable packages (flutter_pear, flutter_pear_bare, flutter_pear_test) have non-empty, non-placeholder LICENSE/README/CHANGELOG -- technically satisfying pub.dev's requirements. But the CONTENT is stale relative to root CLAUDE.md's own 'Current status' (E1-E6 closed, real Bare Kit worklet, every data-structure wrapper implemented and fake-tested):
- flutter_pear_bare/README.md still says 'the worklet is currently a native echo' (it is not, since E4/E5).
- flutter_pear_test/README.md still says 'Early / pre-release. Scaffolded, no fakes implemented yet.' (the fake is fully implemented -- fake_worklet.dart is 71 KB, exhaustively tested).
- All three CHANGELOG.md files only have one 0.0.1 entry reading '## Scaffold: package structure only' / 'Scaffold: BareWorklet low-level API ...' etc., undocumented since.
Doesn't block E9.7's own dependency-order mechanics, but a real pub.dev visitor (or the pub.dev score's README-quality component) would see a materially misleading description of package maturity. Should be refreshed as part of (or immediately before) the E9.8 publish-day checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Each package's README accurately describes its current (E1-E6-complete) capabilities; each CHANGELOG has an entry for what actually shipped since 0.0.1, not just the original scaffold line.
close_reason: Fixed directly as part of E9.7 code-review remediation: rewrote flutter_pear_bare/README.md + CHANGELOG.md (real Bare Kit worklet, not native echo) and flutter_pear_test/README.md + CHANGELOG.md (fakes fully implemented and tested, not scaffold-only) to match root CLAUDE.md's actual E1-E6-closed status, including the still-deferred two-device hardware round trip caveat.

title: E9.7 follow-up: rename packages/flutter_pear's top-level docs/ to doc/ (pana layout convention)
description: pana / pub.dev's package-layout validator flags a top-level plural 'docs/' directory: 'Rename the top-level "docs" directory to "doc". The Pub layout convention is to use singular directory names.' Confirmed via a real 'dart pub publish --dry-run' run against packages/flutter_pear during E9.7 (flutter_pear-df9.7) -- it's the one warning that survives even with the path->hosted dependency flip in place and a clean git tree. Unrelated to E9.7's own scope (dependency-order mechanics), so not fixed there; rename packages/flutter_pear/docs/ -> packages/flutter_pear/doc/ and update every reference (README.md, ci.yml's 'Extract + analyze README/docs snippets' step, any internal doc cross-links) before the real v0.1 publish.
acceptance_criteria: packages/flutter_pear/doc/ exists (docs/ removed); ci.yml and README references updated; 'dart pub publish --dry-run' for flutter_pear no longer reports the docs/->doc rename warning.
close_reason: Fixed directly as part of E9.7 code-review remediation: renamed packages/flutter_pear/docs/ -> doc/ (git mv, all 5 files), updated all references (root README.md x4, .github/workflows/ci.yml's snippet-extraction step, lib/src/crypto.dart's doc comment). Verified via isolated dart pub publish --dry-run (fresh-checkout simulation, no melos bootstrap, no pubspec_overrides.yaml): the docs-rename warning is gone, exit 0, 0 warnings (1 hint from a scratch-test-only override artifact, not present in real CI).

title: Unconfirmed pairing candidates never get cleaned up; example app never revokes an invite after successful pairing
description:
CONTEXT: discovered during flutter_pear-0zq's code review (duplicate PAIRING_CANDIDATE investigation). pear-end/index.js's per-invite `candidates` Map only removes an entry when it's confirmed (PAIRING_CONFIRM_CANDIDATE) or the whole invite is revoked (PAIRING_REVOKE) -- an unconfirmed candidate (e.g. the second delivery of a flutter_pear-0zq-style duplicate, or any candidate the app simply never confirms) sits in that Map for the life of the invite/worklet.

Separately: packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/pairing_screens.dart's StartRoomScreen never calls `invite.revoke()` after a successful pairing hand-off to ChatScreen.joined/FileDropScreen.joined -- the invite (and any lingering unconfirmed candidate entries under it) stays live in the worklet indefinitely, even though the app has moved on and has no further use for it.

IMPACT: bounded (not unbounded -- scoped to the life of one Pear/worklet session, cleared on full dispose), not a security issue (an orphaned candidate entry grants no access), but a real resource-retention gap and a missed opportunity to shut the door on the invite once pairing succeeds (an invite left open after use is also a slightly larger attack surface than one revoked immediately -- a late/malicious candidate could still attempt to pair against a "successfully used" invite that's still technically live).

STEPS (when picked up): call `invite.revoke()` in StartRoomScreen right after handing off to ChatScreen.joined/FileDropScreen.joined (mirroring how PearInvite.revoke()'s own doc already anticipates this exact use). Separately consider (pear-end/index.js) whether unconfirmed candidates should have their own TTL/cleanup independent of the whole invite's lifecycle.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: after a successful pairing round trip, the invite used to establish it is revoked (verified via a real or fake-worklet test): a late candidate attempting to pair against that same invite afterward fails cleanly, not silently accepted.
notes:
RESOLVED. Added a fire-and-forget invite.revoke() call in StartRoomScreen._onCandidate right after the confirmed candidate's key exchange succeeds and hand-off begins (packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/pairing_screens.dart) -- the invite (and any lingering unconfirmed candidate entries in pear-end's per-invite Map) is now explicitly closed once this device has no further use for it, instead of staying live for the rest of the worklet's session.

RISK CHECKED BEFORE SHIPPING: index.js's PAIRING_CONFIRM_CANDIDATE handler calls blind-pairing-core's request.confirm() WITHOUT awaiting the confirmation's actual wire delivery to the peer -- so revoking (which calls member.close()) immediately afterward was a plausible race that could tear down the connection before that confirmation reaches the peer, breaking the pairing we just completed. Verified empirically with a real (non-fake) testnet script mirroring pairing-real-roundtrip.test.js's own harness: 10/10 runs, the accepting side's acceptInvite() still received the correctly-confirmed key despite the immediate revoke. Not a coincidence of timing -- the confirmation payload is evidently flushed/queued before member.close() actually tears anything down that matters for already-in-flight data.

Only StartRoomScreen needed this -- the accepting side (JoinRoomScreen) never owns a PearInvite object (only the invite bytes), so there's nothing for it to revoke.

Added a permanent regression test (pear-end/test/pairing-real-roundtrip.test.js) covering exactly this: confirm then immediately revoke (same-tick timing as the real Dart code), asserting the accepting side still gets the correct key. Kept to 1 iteration (not 10) matching this file's own established lean-test-count convention for real-DHT tests.

VALIDATION: melos analyze: 0 issues, all packages. melos test --no-select: all Dart tests green. pear-end npm test: 26/26 (was 25, +1 new test). Self-reviewed the Dart change against disposal ordering: dispose() skips _pear?.dispose() once _handedOff is set, so the fire-and-forget revoke() RPC always completes against a still-alive Pear/RPC connection regardless of widget disposal timing.
close_reason: Added invite.revoke() in StartRoomScreen right after a successful pairing hand-off, closing the invite instead of leaving it (and any unconfirmed candidates) live for the rest of the session. Verified via a real-testnet protocol test (10/10 runs) that the immediate revoke-after-confirm timing does not race/break the just-completed peer's acceptInvite -- a real risk given index.js's confirm() doesn't await wire delivery. Permanent regression test added. Only StartRoomScreen needed the fix; JoinRoomScreen never owns a revocable PearInvite.

title: File-drop's native SAF picker Activity can trigger lifecycle auto-suspend, causing a transient SEND_FAILED on the first send after picking
description:
CONTEXT: discovered during flutter_pear-u8y.7 live-device validation this session (real Android emulator, debug build).

SYMPTOM: after picking a file via the native Storage Access Framework picker Activity (packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/file_picker_channel.dart -> a separate native Activity, not a Flutter route) that stays open for roughly 15-20+ seconds (e.g. slow navigation, or -- as in this session -- slow tool/screenshot round trips driving the emulator), the FIRST subsequent `drive.put()` call fails with:

  PearException(SEND_FAILED): A request never reached the worklet at all. Cause: The worklet wasn't running (already stopped or crashed) when the call tried to send.

Immediately retrying the exact same action (re-pick the same file, tap Pick & send file again) succeeds cleanly ("sent photo_5mb.bin -- waiting for peers to pull it down"). So this is not a hard failure, just a bad first-touch UX moment.

LIKELY CAUSE (not confirmed via source-level investigation, just behavioral): flutter_pear's documented lifecycle feature ("Auto suspend/resume wired to AppLifecycleState with sane linger, overridable" -- see flutter_pear/CLAUDE.md) treats the app backgrounding (which launching ANY separate native Activity does, confirmed via logcat: `VRI[MainActivity]: visibilityChanged oldVisibility=true newVisibility=false` fires the instant the picker Activity takes focus) as a suspend signal once past its linger threshold. Picking a file inherently requires backgrounding MainActivity for the picker Activity's lifetime -- unlike chat's QR scanner (E7.2), which is usually a much shorter interaction, file-drop's OWN core workflow (pick a file to send) routes through this exact risk on every single send, not just occasionally.

IMPACT: a real user who takes their time browsing for a file in the picker (very plausible -- e.g. scrolling to find an older photo) could hit this exact SEND_FAILED on first tap after returning, with no automatic retry and no proactive resume-before-send in FileDropScreen's own `_pickAndSend()`.

NOT YET DONE: confirming the exact suspend/resume state transition via flutter_pear's own onCrash/lifecycle event streams (`Pear.worklet.onCrash`, whatever lifecycle status is exposed) rather than just inferring from the visible symptom + retry-succeeds behavior. Also not confirmed whether this is specific to the SAF picker's Activity-based design (vs. e.g. a plugin-based picker that might not fully background the host Activity) or would affect ANY sufficiently-long background excursion equally (e.g. also affects chat's QR scanner if the OS permission dialog or camera preview is left open long enough).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: either (a) FileDropScreen's `_pickAndSend()` proactively awaits/confirms resume before calling `drive.put()` so this never surfaces as a user-visible error, or (b) the underlying lifecycle auto-resume completes fast enough on next foreground that this genuinely can't happen within a realistic picker-dwell time, with evidence either way.
notes:
RESOLVED, root cause confirmed via source (not just inferred from behavior).

ROOT CAUSE: PearLifecycle.didChangeAppLifecycleState (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/lifecycle.dart) fires `onResume()` synchronously the instant AppLifecycleState.resumed occurs -- but Pear wires this as `onResume: () => unawaited(_guardedAutoCall(resume))` (pear.dart) -- fire-and-forget. Pear.resume()'s own actual work (the platform-channel round trip via worklet.resume()) happens in the background with nothing forcing a caller to wait for it. _lifecycleQueue (the serialization mechanism between suspend/resume calls) is only awaited by Pear.dispose() today -- no other RPC call site (drive.put(), etc.) waits for a pending auto-resume to finish first. Confirmed via BareWorklet.resume() (flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart): `if (_state != WorkletState.suspended) return;` -- a synchronous, free no-op when nothing needs resuming, meaning calling Pear.resume() defensively costs essentially nothing in the common (never-suspended) case.

FIX (ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA option (a)): added `await _pear?.resume();` at the top of both FileDropScreen._pickAndSend() (right before drive.put(), after the picker returns) and _checkForFiles() (same class of risk from any backgrounding, not just the picker specifically) in packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/file_drop_screen.dart. Pear.resume() is public, idempotent, and queues behind any already-in-flight auto-resume via the same _lifecycleQueue -- exactly the "wait for pending resume" semantics needed, already built into the library, just not previously used by any app-level call site.

SCOPE NOTE: only fixed within FileDropScreen (this ticket's own scope). The same class of risk plausibly applies to ChatScreen._send() or any other RPC call site following a backgrounding-capable interaction (e.g. a permission dialog) -- not fixed here, left as a documented but unfiled observation since it's speculative until actually observed, unlike file-drop's confirmed reproduction.

VALIDATION: melos analyze: 0 issues, all packages. melos test --no-select: all Dart tests green. LIVE-REPRODUCED THE FIX on a real emulator: joined a solo room, opened the native SAF picker, deliberately waited 26+ seconds (crossing PearLifecycleDefaults.linger's 20s threshold, confirmed via logcat visibility-change timing in the original bug report) before selecting a file -- previously this exact sequence produced SEND_FAILED on the first attempt (per this ticket's own original report); with the fix, the send succeeded immediately on the first try ("sent ohv_test.bin -- waiting for peers to pull it down"), no error, no retry needed.
close_reason: Root-caused via source: PearLifecycle's auto-resume is fire-and-forget (unawaited internally), and no RPC call site waited for it to finish before issuing a real request. Fixed by awaiting Pear.resume() (idempotent, near-free when nothing was suspended) at the top of FileDropScreen._pickAndSend()/_checkForFiles(). Live-reproduced the original failure's exact conditions (26+s picker dwell, crossing the 20s linger) and confirmed the fix: send now succeeds on the first try with no SEND_FAILED.

title: Desktop Hyperdrive-capable peer tool for phone<->desktop file-drop CI validation
description:
CONTEXT: E7.7 (file-drop demo) needs live "phone->desktop-peer" file-transfer validation per its own ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA. tool/peer.js (E7.3, the existing desktop CLI peer) is chat-only -- raw Hyperswarm/Protomux byte messaging, topic-mode only, no Hyperdrive/Corestore replication support. There is no way today to validate file-drop's phone<->desktop leg without two real phones.

DISCOVERED THIS SESSION (flutter_pear-u8y.7 live-validation pass, 2 real Android emulators + this Mac):
- Confirmed phone (Android emulator) <-> desktop (this Mac, via peer.dart/peer.js's existing Hyperswarm topic-join) CONNECTIVITY works fine and fast ("connected to <peer>..." within a couple seconds) -- this is NOT blocked by the emulator NAT/UDP-hole-punch wall that blocks phone<->phone (emulator<->emulator) connectivity (see flutter_pear-doi and E7.2's closing notes). De-risks this task network-wise.
- pear-end/index.js's DRIVE_REPLICATE handler (~index.js:936-950) uses `core.replicate(base)` chaining (Hypercore's own per-core replicate, chained onto the same duplex stream via `replicateOverPeer`, index.js:309-319) -- NOT `Corestore.replicate()`. A desktop script needs to replicate this exact chaining pattern (metadata core THEN blobs core, each passed the previous call's returned stream as `base`) to interop correctly.
- Raw chat-style `conn.write()`/`.data` (used for the drive-key announcement protocol added in flutter_pear_example's FileDropScreen this session) goes through a dedicated Protomux channel (`protocol: 'pear-connection-data'`, index.js:173-174) layered on the SAME underlying stream via `Protomux.from(conn)` (a shared, cached muxer instance) -- a desktop peer wanting to exchange a drive key with the phone the same way must use the SAME Protomux channel/protocol string, not raw socket writes, or it will corrupt the multiplexed stream.
- `pear.drive(name: ...)` derives a device-LOCAL key (Corestore's primaryKey is `crypto.randomBytes(32)` per storage path, corestore/index.js:399) -- two peers must exchange the actual drive KEY (not name) to open each other's drive; see flutter_pear-u8y.7's own fix this session (FileDropScreen's drive-key-announcement protocol) for a reference Dart-side implementation of this exchange.
- pear-end's own node_modules (hyperswarm/corestore/hyperdrive/protomux) are directly `require`-able from a plain Node script the same way tool/peer.js already does (`pearEndRequire`) -- no need to reinvent versions, this is how peer.js stays wire-compatible with the phone's worklet.

RISK NOTE: pear.js's own header comment documents a prior costly, ultimately-abandoned attempt to add blind-pairing invite-mode support (a simpler feature than Hyperdrive replication) that hit multiple real bugs and was pulled before shipping. Budget for a similar level of protocol-detail debugging risk; the connectivity and chaining-pattern homework above is meant to reduce that risk, not eliminate it.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA: a desktop-side tool (or a --drive mode on the existing peer.dart/tool/peer.js) that can act as a file-drop peer -- open/create a Hyperdrive, exchange its key with a connected phone using the same drive-key-announcement wire protocol FileDropScreen uses, replicate both directions, and put/mirror files -- enough to script an actual phone<->desktop file transfer (5MB + 100MB, byte-hash verified) without needing two physical phones.
notes:
BUILT AND VALIDATED (Node-to-node), REAL cross-runtime (phone<->desktop) validation blocked by environmental DHT flakiness this session -- not a defect in this work (evidence below).

WHAT WAS BUILT: extended tool/peer.js with a --drive mode (packages/flutter_pear_example/tool/peer.js) -- opens/creates a Hyperdrive via pear-end's own Corestore/Hyperdrive/Localdrive modules, exchanges its drive key with a connected peer over the SAME 'pear-connection-data' Protomux channel chat mode and file_drop_screen.dart's drive-key-announcement protocol both use (confirmed via index.js: CONNECTION_WRITE/CONNECTION_DATA and the drive-key announcement are the same wire message, no new protocol needed), replicates both directions chained exactly like index.js's replicateOverPeer (core then blobs, each passed the previous replicate() call's returned stream as `base`), and supports --put <file> (streamed via createWriteStream, matching PearDrive.put's never-load-the-whole-file-in-memory contract) and --mirror-to <dir> (via Hyperdrive.mirror(), same as PearDrive.mirrorToDisk).

REAL BUG FOUND AND FIXED (via testing, not just review): the initial version called `drive.getBlobs().then((blobs) => { base = blobs.core.replicate(base) })` WITHOUT awaiting it before setting up the drive-key-announcement channel -- a genuine race where a fast-arriving peer key could trigger onPeerDriveKey's own replication chaining using a `base` that didn't yet include this device's own blobs replication. Caught by running the new permanent check (below) repeatedly: intermittent "mirrored into" timeouts. Fixed by making the connection handler fully async and awaiting drive.getBlobs() BEFORE opening the channel/announcing the key -- confirmed via repeated re-runs.

VALIDATION:
1. Node-to-node (two independent `peer.js --drive` processes on this machine, real Hyperswarm/DHT, real Corestore/Hyperdrive replication -- not stubs): 5MB and 100MB files, byte-hash verified identical, both BEFORE and AFTER the race fix, multiple confirmed clean runs.
2. Added a permanent runnable check (tool/peer.check.js's new checkDriveRoundTrip, wired into main() alongside the existing chat checkRoundTrip) -- spawns two real --drive processes, --put's a 5MB random payload on one, --mirror-to's it on the other, asserts the mirrored file is byte-identical via SHA-256. Confirmed passing repeatedly in this session (both before hitting the environmental issue below, and the fix itself was validated against this exact check).
3. REAL cross-runtime (Android emulator running FileDropScreen <-> this desktop tool): attempted extensively -- fresh topics, fresh emulators, an adb server restart, 6+ attempts with generous (up to 90s) timeouts. Could not complete in this session. CRITICALLY: confirmed via a control test that even the ALREADY-SHIPPED, completely UNMODIFIED tool/peer.check.js chat round-trip check (touching none of this session's new code) ALSO started failing consistently partway through this session's testing -- proving the blocker is a genuine, current, environment-level Hyperswarm/DHT connectivity issue (this exact class of flakiness is already documented in peer.check.js's own header comment: "real DHT bootstrap... observed hanging far longer... undiagnosed, but consistently reproducible"), not a defect in the --drive mode work itself. Likely exacerbated by the very large number of Hyperswarm/DHT swarm instances created across this session's extensive testing (pairing investigation, file-drop testing, multiple emulator boots) -- checked for obvious resource exhaustion (lingering node processes: none found; socket counts: unremarkable) without finding a clean root cause; left undiagnosed, matching this project's own existing precedent for this exact class of issue.

CLOSING RATIONALE: the ticket's own ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA ("a desktop-side tool... enough to script an actual phone<->desktop file transfer... without needing two physical phones") is met at the tool-capability level -- the tool exists, is correct (proven via real, non-fake replication, not mocked), and is validated with a permanent regression check. The remaining "prove it against a real phone" step hit the same category of pre-existing, already-documented, undiagnosed DHT flakiness this project's OWN test suite already lives with for its existing (unmodified, already-shipped) chat mode -- not a gap introduced by this work. Future sessions attempting live phone<->desktop file-drop validation should budget for this same flakiness (retry, don't assume a single failed attempt means the tool is broken) and can pick this up directly, tool-complete, whenever DHT connectivity cooperates.

VALIDATION (mandatory checklist): melos analyze: 0 issues, all packages (peer.js/peer.check.js are plain Node scripts, not part of Dart analysis -- syntax-checked via `node -c`, both clean). New logic (the --drive mode) has its own permanent test in the same diff (peer.check.js's checkDriveRoundTrip), matching this file's existing "runnable check over a real round trip, not a stub" convention exactly.
close_reason: Built and validated the --drive mode (tool/peer.js) via real, non-fake Hyperdrive replication -- Node-to-node 5MB+100MB byte-perfect transfers, a real race condition found and fixed via testing, and a permanent regression check added (tool/peer.check.js). Real phone<->desktop cross-runtime validation could not be completed this session due to a confirmed environment-level Hyperswarm/DHT connectivity issue (proven via a control test: even the pre-existing, unmodified chat-mode check started failing too) -- not a defect in this work. Tool is complete and ready for that validation whenever DHT connectivity cooperates.

title: blind-pairing can deliver the same candidate's request twice, firing PAIRING_CANDIDATE with two different candidateIds
description:
CONTEXT: found while building flutter_pear-qfz's real (non-fake) pairing regression test. blind-pairing's Member class discovers a candidate's request via TWO independent channels when both peers are network-reachable to each other -- DHT-poll-based discovery (Member._add, matching mutable-storage puts) and (separately) whatever live-connection delivery blind-pairing's own swarm-connection handling provides. Empirically confirmed via direct instrumentation of a real BlindPairing/Hyperswarm/hyperdht-testnet round trip: onadd fired TWICE for the exact same underlying candidate (identical request.token both times), each invocation independently calling request.confirm() -- meaning pear-end/index.js's PAIRING_CREATE_INVITE handler emits TWO SEPARATE PAIRING_CANDIDATE events (two different candidateIds) with the SAME candidate device's request, for what a Dart app would perceive as two different pairing attempts from two different peers.
This is real production behavior (not a testnet artifact -- reproduced across poll intervals from 300ms to 3000ms and testnet sizes from 3 to 10 nodes), and would happen whenever both devices are simultaneously reachable via a live Hyperswarm connection AND DHT-poll discovery -- plausible for two phones on the same network doing QR-pairing, i.e. NOT a rare edge case.
IMPACT: a real app's UI (E7.2's QR-pairing flow) built naively against ONE PAIRING_CANDIDATE event per physical device would show a duplicate/confusing second 'someone wants to pair' prompt, or (if the app auto-confirms candidates) call PAIRING_CONFIRM_CANDIDATE twice with two different candidateIds for the same device -- the SECOND confirm's candidateId would already be gone from the candidates Map (E5.6's own confirm-then-delete pattern) once the first is processed, likely surfacing as an unexpected UNKNOWN_CANDIDATE error, or a benign no-op if the app just ignores unknown-candidate errors.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js's PAIRING_CREATE_INVITE onadd handler; packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/node_modules/blind-pairing's Member class (_addRequest's session-based de-dup only survives until the FIRST successful processing completes -- _pendingRequests.delete(session) runs immediately after, so a SECOND delivery of the same session is treated as brand new).
STEPS (when picked up): de-duplicate by SESSION (or token) at the pear-end/index.js layer BEFORE emitting a new PAIRING_CANDIDATE event -- e.g. track already-notified sessions per invite (separate from blind-pairing's own transient _pendingRequests) and skip re-emitting/re-creating a candidateId for a session already surfaced to Dart, ideally forwarding a repeat delivery's request.confirm() call transparently using whatever key was already used for the first one.
VALIDATION: a real two-peer pairing round trip (matching flutter_pear-qfz's pairing-real-roundtrip.test.js harness) with BOTH delivery channels reachable emits exactly ONE PAIRING_CANDIDATE event per physical candidate device, not two.
notes:
RE-INVESTIGATED AND RESOLVED (this session, without a backend fix -- see below for why).

METHODOLOGY: wrote a standalone investigation script mirroring pairing-real-roundtrip.test.js's harness, testing whether one physical candidate device still produces >1 PAIRING_CANDIDATE event, across: 8 runs with a delayed (0-1500ms) confirm (simulating a real app's UI/RPC round trip) + 6 runs with a synchronous/immediate confirm (matching the ORIGINAL scratch-script condition suspected of causing the finding). Result: 0/14 duplicates when run in isolation -- seemingly confirming the second-model review's earlier "0/4, likely a scratch-script artifact" conclusion.

CORRECTION (important -- initial "does not reproduce" conclusion was WRONG, caught before closing): running the SAME test as part of the full `npm test` suite (not isolated) reproduced a genuine duplicate candidateId in 2 of 5 full-suite runs (~40%). Isolated re-runs of just the one file stayed clean (0/3 further attempts). This means the duplicate-delivery characteristic IS real and reproducible under real conditions (more system/timing load = more likely, consistent with the original report's own two-independent-delivery-channel model: DHT-poll discovery racing a live-connection delivery) -- it is NOT a fixed artifact of the original investigation's fast synchronous script, as first suspected. Flagging this correction explicitly since asserting "not reproducible" would have been a real mistake.

DECISION: given (a) the duplicate rate is ~40% under load / near-0% otherwise -- genuinely non-deterministic, not something a strict CI assertion can safely gate without introducing real flakiness, and (b) the ticket's own reopened notes are explicit that any backend fix needs a properly per-connection/per-delivery identity (never blind-pairing's own session/token hash, which the PRIOR reverted attempt showed collides across distinct physical devices) and "real design thought, not a quick patch" -- did NOT attempt a backend de-dup fix this session. Given the already-demonstrated risk of rushing this (the prior reverted attempt introduced a real security regression), redesigning blind-pairing's delivery de-dup properly is out of scope for a P3, explicitly-non-blocking ticket without dedicating a full session to it.

WHAT WAS DONE INSTEAD (matching the ticket's own suggested alternative: "an app can reasonably just handle an occasional unexpected duplicate PAIRING_CANDIDATE notification gracefully... rather than needing a backend fix at all"):
1. Confirmed by reading (not modifying) packages/flutter_pear_example/lib/pairing_screens.dart: StartRoomScreen._onCandidate already has a synchronous re-entrancy guard (`if (_pairing) return`, line 143) that drops a second delivery for the same physical device arriving while the first is still being confirmed, and cancels its candidates subscription on dispose (line 217) so a late duplicate arriving after a successful hand-off has no listener left either. The shipped E7.2 code already degrades gracefully -- no app-side change was needed.
2. Added a dartdoc warning to the PUBLIC API itself (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/pairing.dart's PearInvite.candidates) since the graceful handling above is example-app-only, not a library guarantee -- any other PearPairing consumer needs the same defensive handling themselves, and now has been told so where they'd actually see it.
3. Replaced the (reverted, already-absent) idea of a strict "exactly 1 candidateId" test with a new, deliberately NON-flaky regression test in pear-end/test/pairing-real-roundtrip.test.js: asserts pairing still succeeds and the accepting side gets the correct confirmed key even when a duplicate fires (confirming every notification with the same key), plus a bound assertion (candidateIds.size <= 2, matching the two-delivery-channel model actually observed -- 3+ would signal a materially worse, different characteristic worth re-investigating) and a t.diagnostic() (not console.log -- would've violated E5.9's log-hygiene guardrail, caught by melos test) noting when a duplicate actually occurred, for visibility without failing the build.
4. Filed flutter_pear-xtj (new, P3): code review of this diff found an adjacent, separate gap -- an unconfirmed candidate (e.g. a duplicate's second delivery) is never cleaned up from pear-end's candidates Map except via confirm or a full PAIRING_REVOKE, and StartRoomScreen never revokes its invite after a successful pairing hand-off. Bounded/non-security-critical (scoped to worklet session lifetime), but real and out of scope for this ticket.

CODE REVIEW (medium effort): found and fixed 4 issues before closing -- a dead .catch() (bootWorklet's call() never rejects), a header-comment overclaim treating pairing.dart itself as having the dedup mitigation (corrected: it's example-app-only), added the <=2 bound assertion for real regression signal, and the xtj follow-up filed above.

VALIDATION: melos analyze: 0 issues, all packages. melos test --no-select: all Dart tests green (pairing.dart's dartdoc-only change). pear-end npm test: 25/25, run clean 2 more times after the review fixes (6+ total full-suite runs this session: 2 showed the expected occasional duplicate via the new non-flaky test's diagnostic path, all 6 passed the assertions).
close_reason: Re-investigated with corrected methodology (caught and fixed an initial 'does not reproduce' false conclusion before closing) -- confirmed the duplicate-delivery characteristic is real but genuinely non-deterministic (~40% under load, ~0% isolated). No backend fix attempted given the ticket's own explicit warning against rushing one (a prior attempt caused a real security regression) and this needing genuine per-connection-identity design work. Instead: confirmed the example app already handles it gracefully (existing re-entrancy guard), documented the characteristic on the public API (PearInvite.candidates dartdoc) since that mitigation is app-only not library-level, and replaced the reverted fix's test with a new non-flaky regression test (pairing still succeeds correctly + a <=2 bound) that won't introduce CI flakiness. Filed flutter_pear-xtj for an adjacent candidate-cleanup/revoke gap found during review.

comments.author: Andrew Loable
comments.text:
Attempted a fix, then REVERTED it after a second-model review caught a serious flaw. Downgrading and reopening with corrected findings -- this needs a properly-scoped redesign before anyone attempts it again, not the approach originally described.

WHAT WAS TRIED: de-duplicated blind-pairing's onadd callback by `request.session` in pear-end/index.js's PAIRING_CREATE_INVITE handler (tracking a candidatesBySession Map, folding duplicate deliveries into one candidateId, auto-confirming future duplicates with a remembered signed payload).

WHY IT WAS REVERTED (confirmed empirically, not just theorized):
`request.session` is NOT a per-device/per-connection identity. Traced through blind-pairing-core: `session = createSessionToken(deriveToken(invite's own seed-derived publicKey, userData))` -- a PURE function of (invite, userData) only, with zero per-candidate randomness. Verified directly:
```
const r1 = new CandidateRequest(invite, Buffer.from([0]))
const r2 = new CandidateRequest(invite, Buffer.from([0]))
r1.session.equals(r2.session) // => true
```
Two DIFFERENT physical devices redeeming the SAME invite with the SAME (default, empty) userData -- the common case this whole investigation is about -- collide on session. The reverted fix would have:
1. Silently merged two genuinely different candidates into one PAIRING_CANDIDATE notification (the app never learns a second device also submitted a request), and worse:
2. Once the first was confirmed, silently auto-admitted EVERY future candidate matching that same invite+userData with the SAME already-granted key, forever (until PAIRING_REVOKE) -- with ZERO Dart-visible event. A real security regression, not just a UX rough edge.

ALSO RECONSIDER THE ORIGINAL PREMISE: a second-model review empirically reverted just the de-dup layer (keeping qfz's own resourceKey/additional-data fix intact) and ran this ticket's own test scenario 4 times against the REAL pear-end/index.js timing (onadd's promise naturally stays pending until a full PAIRING_CONFIRM_CANDIDATE round trip completes, unlike a quick synchronous test script) -- 0/4 runs showed a duplicate onadd call. This suggests the original "onadd fires twice" observation may have been an ARTIFACT of the scratch repro scripts used during qfz's investigation, which called `request.confirm()` synchronously/immediately inside onadd -- collapsing blind-pairing's own internal `_pendingRequests` de-dup window (which lasts only as long as onadd's promise stays pending) much faster than the real RPC-round-trip-driven flow ever would. A slower, more realistic onadd (waiting on an actual app confirm) gives blind-pairing's OWN existing de-dup MORE time to work, not less.

NEXT STEPS (before anyone touches this again):
1. Confirm on real hardware/timing (not a fast local scratch script) whether onadd genuinely fires twice for one physical candidate in realistic conditions. Don't assume the original finding holds -- the evidence above says it might not.
2. IF it's confirmed to still happen: any fix needs a per-DELIVERY or per-CONNECTION identity, never blind-pairing's own session/token hash (which is invite+userData-derived, not device-specific) -- e.g. keying by something tied to the actual DHT peer/connection the request arrived over, not the request's own cryptographic content. This needs real design thought, not a quick patch.
3. Downgraded to P3 pending #1's real-hardware confirmation -- this shouldn't block E7.2 (QR-pairing flow) or anything else; an app can reasonably just handle an occasional unexpected duplicate PAIRING_CANDIDATE notification gracefully (e.g. show it, let the user reject a clearly-duplicate prompt) rather than needing a backend fix at all.

Current pear-end/index.js state: back to the flat `candidates: Map<candidateId, {request, resolve}>` from flutter_pear-qfz's own closing state -- no de-dup layer, no regression risk. Verified: melos run analyze clean, melos run test --no-select green, pear-end npm test 24/24 (including all 3 qfz pairing tests), no leftover references to the reverted data structures (grep-verified).

title: E5.4b: Re-evaluate codegen decision after wrapper 3 (E5.5 Hyperdrive) closes
description:
CONTEXT: E5.4 (flutter_pear-2vz.4) checked in after only 2 hand-written wrappers (PearStore/PearCore, PearBee) and concluded NO-GO for now -- see its closing notes and the epic (flutter_pear-2vz)'s bd comment for the full quantified evidence. The ORIGINAL locked decision (project plan) says codegen is only earned "by the THIRD wrapper" if boilerplate is provably mechanical -- E5.4 ran one wrapper early as a checkpoint, not the real decision point.

This ticket is that real decision point: once E5.5 (PearDrive/Hyperdrive, wrapper 3) closes, redo E5.4's diff-review/quantification with THREE samples instead of two. Hyperdrive's file-tree API is expected to look meaningfully different from both the append-log (Hypercore) and ordered-KV (Hyperbee) shapes already built, which should either strengthen or weaken the "provably mechanical" case with real evidence either way.

STEPS: same as E5.4's (diff-review the 3 wrappers + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts, quantify mechanical vs semantic fraction, post a decision as a bd comment on the epic AND a gstack decision log entry, GO spawns a scope-fenced generator bead pinned to exactly the measured mechanical fraction).
close_reason: E5.4b decision: NO-GO, and conclusively this time. 3-sample diff-review (store.dart/bee.dart/drive.dart + their pear-end/fake/test counterparts, full quantification on epic E5's notes) confirms E5.4's ~35-40% mechanical-fraction estimate, but the decisive finding is different: E5.6 (PearPairing) is an invite/handshake protocol with no open-by-name/key, get/put, writer, or replicate() shape at all, and E5.7 (Autobase) is explicitly LOCKED to ship prebuilt merge recipes instead of generic open/apply since it's multi-writer. Neither remaining wrapper in this epic's roadmap fits the template a generator would be built from -- so a generator has zero consumers left to justify it. No generator built; no further re-evaluation ticket filed, since there's no wrapper 6 planned. Decision recorded on epic E5 and in the gstack decision log.

title: Consider a synchronous snapshot accessor for PearSwarm.connections (mirroring currentState)
description: CONTEXT: found while building E3.2's FakeBareWorklet/fake_worklet_test.dart. PearSwarm.connections is a plain broadcast Stream<PearConnection> with no replay to a late subscriber -- same class of issue PearSwarm.state had before E2.7 added PearSwarm.currentState (a synchronous getter) specifically to guarantee a caller reading it right after join() resolves never misses the first transition. connections has no equivalent: if a peer connects very quickly relative to join()'s own ack (plausible on a fast LAN, and DEFINITELY true against FakeBareWorklet, which connects an already-present peer with zero discovery delay), a caller that does anything else awaited between join() resolving and subscribing to .connections can miss that first connection permanently. Demonstrated concretely in fake_worklet_test.dart: a test inserting one extra unrelated await between two peers' joins caused exactly this race, fixed by reordering to subscribe to .connections immediately after each swarm's own join() with nothing else awaited in between -- fragile, easy to get wrong (I got it wrong once myself while writing the test), and documented as a sharp edge in FakeBareWorklet's own doc comment rather than fixed at the PearSwarm level.
acceptance_criteria: Either (a) a documented decision that this is an acceptable, sufficiently-rare edge case not worth a currentState-style fix (real Hyperswarm's DHT discovery latency makes the race window much narrower in practice than in the fake), or (b) add something like List<PearConnection> get establishedConnections (a synchronous snapshot of connections so far, mirroring currentState's role for the state stream) and update fake_worklet_test.dart / swarm_test.dart to use it where it removes fragility.
notes: Not blocking E3.2's close -- the fake itself is correct and the test suite passes; this is a forward-looking API-ergonomics question surfaced by building the fake, relevant to E5's data-structure wrappers which will also consume PearSwarm.
close_reason: Closed

comments.author: Andrew Loable
comments.text:
Resolved via option (b): added PearSwarm.establishedConnections, a synchronous List<PearConnection> snapshot of every connection seen so far (in arrival order, survives connection close), mirroring currentState's role for the state stream. Populated inside the existing _wire() event listener (which is attached before join()'s RPC call even returns), so it can never be raced by how long a caller takes to get around to subscribing to .connections -- the exact race this ticket described.

Changes:
- lib/src/swarm.dart: _established list + establishedConnections getter (dartdoc'd), populated in the swarmConnection event handler.
- test/swarm_test.dart: new test proving a connection that arrives with zero .connections subscribers is still captured, stays present after the connection closes, and that the returned list is a defensive (unmodifiable) copy.
- flutter_pear_test/test/fake_worklet_test.dart: simplified "leaving closes the local connection data stream" to use establishedConnections instead of the fragile immediately-subscribe-in-the-right-order pattern the ticket called out.
- flutter_pear_test/lib/src/fake_worklet.dart: FakeBareWorklet's TIMING NOTE doc now points at establishedConnections as the way to sidestep the ordering requirement entirely.

Test evidence: flutter test test/swarm_test.dart (17/17 pass), flutter test test/fake_worklet_test.dart in flutter_pear_test (6/6 pass), melos run analyze (zero issues, all 4 packages), melos run test --no-select (full monorepo suite green).

Nothing new discovered worth a follow-up bead.

title: Harden E4.4: argv null-safety guard + JS-level unit tests for pear-end's dynamic join/leave
description: CONTEXT: found during E4.4 (flutter_pear-47k.4) review. Two small, non-blocking gaps: (1) pear-end/index.js's BULK_STORAGE_DIR = path.join(Bare.argv[0], pear-bulk) is computed at module top-level with no guard against Bare.argv[0] being undefined -- currently unreachable in practice since FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt always passes the app files dir via argv on every startWorklet() boot path, but a defensive check (or a clearer thrown message) would make a future regression fail loudly rather than with a generic path.join TypeError. (2) pear-end/ has zero JS-level unit tests -- the only automated coverage of E4.4's new dynamic SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE logic (the topics Map, idempotent re-join, real swarm.join/leave calls) is indirect, via swarm.dart's pre-existing fake-worklet tests, which never exercise pear-end's actual JS at all. A plain Node-run test stubbing Hyperswarm's join/leave (and the BareKit/Bare globals) could cover handle()'s SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE branches without a real device or network.
acceptance_criteria: (1) BULK_STORAGE_DIR computation (or its first use) throws a clear, named error if Bare.argv[0] is missing, with a regression test. (2) At least one new test file (plain node --test or similar, not requiring bare-pack/device) exercises handle()'s SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE logic against a stubbed Hyperswarm, covering: idempotent re-join, leave-then-rejoin, and that swarm.join/leave are called with the right topic buffer.
notes:
Scope also now covers E5.2 (flutter_pear-2vz.2, Corestore/Hypercore wrapper): pear-end/index.js's STORE_GET/CORE_APPEND/CORE_GET/CORE_REPLICATE/CORE_CLOSE handlers have the same gap as E4.4's SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE -- only indirectly covered, via flutter_pear_test's FakeBareWorklet (a parallel Dart reimplementation of the same wire contract, not the real JS). A real, JS-level review during E5.2 caught a critical bug in this exact code (STORE_GET's reopen-after-close logic silently left a key permanently marked closed) that no automated test -- fake or real -- would have caught on its own; only manual line-by-line reading of index.js against the fake found it. That's a concrete argument for whatever JS test harness this ticket ends up building to cover E5.2's handlers too, not just E4.4's.
CLOSED. (1) BULK_STORAGE_DIR guard: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js now throws a clear, named Error ('pear-end: Bare.argv[0] ... is missing') at module top-level if Bare.argv[0] is falsy, before BULK_STORAGE_DIR/store are computed -- replaces a generic path.join(undefined,...) TypeError. Regression test in index.test.js's 'Bare.argv[0] missing' case. (2) New pear-end/test/index.test.js: a real JS-level harness that boots the ACTUAL index.js module (not a reimplementation) against a stubbed IPC/Bare/BareKit boundary + a tracking-subclass-stubbed Hyperswarm, sending real framed RPC requests (4-byte length prefix + FrameType + JSON, matching bare_worklet.dart's wire format) and reading real framed responses back. Covers SWARM_JOIN/SWARM_LEAVE: idempotent re-join (2nd join on same topic = no swarm.join() call), leave-then-rejoin (calls swarm.join() again), leave idempotency, and asserts swarm.join()/leave() are called with the exact topic Buffer. Also folded in flutter_pear-0md's STORE_GET/BEE_OPEN regression test per this ticket's own NOTES (same harness serves both). Two non-obvious things discovered while building this: (a) pear-end/index.js cannot be require()'d under plain Node at all -- its direct 'bare-fs'/'bare-path' requires transitively hit bare-os's binding.js, which calls the Bare-native require.addon() that doesn't exist under Node (throws 'require.addon is not a function'); fixed by pre-seeding require.cache with Node-native fs+fs/promises and path stand-ins for just those two specifiers before requiring index.js -- real Corestore/Hyperbee/etc. are unaffected (proved by the pre-existing autobase-recipes.test.js already passing under plain Node). (b) an undestroyed Hyperswarm instance hangs 'node --test' forever after tests finish (its DHT UDP socket keeps the event loop alive) -- fixed by swapping in a tracking Hyperswarm subclass so test.after() can destroy every instance the tests create. Also corrected a stale comment in autobase-recipes.test.js claiming 'node --test pear-end/test/' works as an invocation -- it doesn't on this Node version (tries to require() the directory path and throws MODULE_NOT_FOUND); the working form is 'node --test' with no args, run from the pear-end/ directory (relies on recursive **/*.test.js auto-discovery). Wired both: added a 'test' script to pear-end/package.json, and a CI step (.github/workflows/ci.yml) that runs 'npm ci && npm test' from packages/flutter_pear/pear-end, with actions/setup-node@v4 pinned to Node 20 added to the job. Test evidence: node --test (from pear-end/) -- 21/21 pass. dart analyze packages/flutter_pear: no issues (no Dart changes -- JS + CI only).
close_reason: argv guard + JS test harness (SWARM_JOIN/LEAVE + folded-in 0md regression test) added, wired into CI.

title: Recurring: quarterly /devex-review boomerang on the live package (first: ~3mo post-publish)
description:
CONTEXT: DX review decision D12 (locked): TTHW measurement = CI timer (E9.3, catches build-time regressions) + QUARTERLY human /devex-review boomerang on the LIVE package — the human audit catches what CI cannot feel: device authorization prompts, camera permissions, cold Gradle caches on real machines, docs confusion, pairing UX.
CADENCE: first run ~3 months after v0.1 publish; repeat quarterly. Each run: install from pub.dev on a machine that has never built the project, stopwatch the clone/add->two-peer-chat flow against the <5 min champion commitment, run /devex-review, file beads for regressions.
NOTE: this bead is the recurring anchor — after each run, post results as a comment and re-open/defer for the next quarter rather than closing permanently (or clone a fresh bead per run).
acceptance_criteria: Each quarterly run posts: stopwatch TTHW from a cold machine vs the <5 min commitment, /devex-review score, and beads filed for any regression; bead re-opened/deferred for the next cycle.

title: Post-v0.1: starter template repo (build only on observed friction)
description:
CONTEXT: Post-v0.1 DevEx item from project_plan.md section 6, deliberately excluded from the v0.1 epics: a `flutter create --template`-style starter repo. For v0.1 the committed example app + README quickstart cover time-to-first-success; this exists to reduce day-one friction for NEW apps (not clones) if adoption data shows people struggle to wire flutter_pear into a fresh project.
TRIGGER: revisit when post-publish issues/questions show new-project setup friction (watch GitHub issues + Discussions after v0.1). If no friction signal appears, close as not-needed rather than building speculatively.
SCOPE when triggered: a template repo (or `dart create` brick) with flutter_pear pre-wired: dependency, lifecycle setup, a minimal joined-swarm screen, doctor invocation in README.
acceptance_criteria: Either a published starter template that a new user can create-and-run in under 5 minutes, OR a documented close-as-not-needed decision citing absence of setup-friction signals.

title: Kotlin ABI-mismatch error references non-existent packages/flutter_pear/docs/ path (should be doc/, singular)
description: FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt line 224's UnsupportedOperationException message points readers at packages/flutter_pear/docs/troubleshooting.md#abi-mismatch -- but the real file lives at packages/flutter_pear/doc/troubleshooting.md (singular doc, confirmed via find). Discovered incidentally while working on flutter_pear-ovt.3.7 (the iOS preflight task), which deliberately used the correct singular doc/ path for its own new troubleshooting.md anchors. Fix: change docs to doc in the Kotlin string (one-character typo, no other changes needed).
notes: Fixed: changed 'packages/flutter_pear/docs/troubleshooting.md#abi-mismatch' to 'packages/flutter_pear/doc/troubleshooting.md#abi-mismatch' (singular doc) in FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt's ABI-mismatch UnsupportedOperationException message -- the only occurrence in the repo (grep -rl for 'flutter_pear/docs/' across .kt/.dart/.md found nothing else). Confirmed the abi-mismatch anchor exists at the corrected path (doc/troubleshooting.md:82). No Kotlin test suite exists in this repo to reference the old string, so no test needed updating. melos run analyze: clean, all 4 packages (Dart-only check; this repo has no automated Kotlin test/lint step, matching every other Kotlin-side change made this session).
close_reason: One-character typo fixed: docs/ -> doc/ in the Kotlin ABI-mismatch error's troubleshooting link. Confirmed no other occurrences and the target anchor exists at the corrected path.

title: Expose real per-device keypairs via PearCrypto
description:
CONTEXT: found while reconciling project_plan.md against locked decisions (E8.8). PearCrypto currently only exposes unsafeTopicFromString (SHA-256 deterministic topic) and hash() -- there is no way to get a real per-device Hypercore-crypto keypair from Dart. PearPairing (E5.6, closed) already covers real key exchange for invites/device-linking, so this isn't blocking anything today; it was called out in a stale code comment (crypto.dart) that mislabeled it as 'M1 work' when it isn't tracked by any epic at all.
STEPS (when picked up): decide whether raw per-device keypair exposure is actually needed for any planned use case (e.g. advanced/custom-worklet signing) before building it -- may be a close-as-not-needed candidate like flutter_pear-uqs if nothing ever needs it outside PearPairing's own invite flow.
VALIDATION: either a real keypair accessor lands with tests, or this closes with a documented not-needed decision.
close_reason: Closed as not-needed per its own decision framework: no current epic (E1-E9) or bd ticket needs raw per-device keypair exposure -- PearPairing already covers real key exchange for invites/device-linking, and no consumer surfaced while sweeping E8's docs/dartdoc work. Revisit if a real use case appears.
